1. MARINE NAVIGATION 
 
Navigation is a field of study that focuses on the process of monitoring and controlling the 
movement of a craft or vehicle from one place to another. The field of navigation includes 
four general categories: land navigation, marine navigation, aeronautic navigation, and 
space navigation. 
Marine navigation, depending on the area of navigation, is divided into coastal navigation, 
ocean navigation, and polar navigation. 
Coastal navigation takes place at sea or coastal zone in sight of the coast and applies to 
the terrestrial navigation system. The terrestrial navigation system includes a method of 
keeping the ship and the ship positioning by observation of natural or artificial objects, or 
sea depth measurement. 
Ocean navigation takes place in sailing away from the coast when the coast is not visible 
and we must apply a celestial navigation system. 
The celestial navigation includes a method of determining ships position by observation of 
the heavenly bodies (Sun, Moon, stars, and planets). 
Polar navigation takes place in the Polar Regions. 
In maritime navigation, we use different navigation methods for determining the ship's 
position. 
In addition to terrestrial coastal navigation, navigation systems used are: electronic 
navigation systems comprising of a radio navigation system, satellite navigation system, and 
radar navigation system. 
 
The navigation tasks 
 
For safe navigation, it is necessary to systematically and responsibly solve navigation tasks 
and check the accuracy of navigational instruments. Boat leader category C (navigator) is 
the qualified person performing the navigation service. 
 
The main tasks of a skipper in navigation are: 
 
➢ Operate a boat/yacht safely without endangering people, boats, and environment 
➢ Determine the course and distance boat/yacht must cross in that course or to determine 
the time of travel between the two positions, 
➢ To determine the position of the boat, check the course and speed of the boat, 
➢ Keep good lookout (visually and/or by radar) and be aware of the environment. 
 
Navigation aids 
 
To solve the above tasks we can use these navigation aids: 
➢ compass, an instrument for orientation, maintain the course of the boat and measuring 
the azimuth (bearing) of visible objects, 
➢ speedometer, for measuring the speed or the number of miles traveled in a period of time, 
➢ depth gauge for measuring the sea depth 
➢ watch (chronometer) for knowing the exact time, note - if the ship has a chronometer then 
we must keep a log and know it’s error 
➢ electronic navigation systems (radar, satellite navigation system), 
➢ maritime chart, a basic tool for orientation and graphical solve of the navigation tasks,

➢ Navigation equipment, supplies for graphic solving of the navigation tasks (nautical 
triangle, plotter, compass, graphical pencil, eraser and etc.). 
➢ informative chart, gives a number of useful data for the east coast of the Adriatic 
Sea and 
➢ navigational manuals, give a description of the navigable areas and other important 
information for safe navigation that could not be recorded on a chart. 
 
1.1. NAUTICAL CHART 
 
1.1.1. The shape and size of the Earth 
 
Earth is a planet in the solar system seen as a ball until in the 17th century has not been 
established that the shape is ellipsoid.  
To solve navigation tasks navigator is required to have the image of the part of the Earth's 
surface with all the features important for orientation and navigation safety of the ship. 
 
 
Picture - Surface of the Earth as a sphere on paper 
 
Earth as a sphere cannot be spread evenly in the plane, so we do this by using the chart 
projection mapping the Earth's surface to the geometric body whose shell can be spread 
evenly in the plane. There are different projections of the Earth, but for navigation, we are 
using only those projections that allow a quick and easy way to solve navigation tasks. 
 
For practical uses, the navigation projection of the Earth's surface is from the center of the 
Earth to the surface of the cylinder. If cylinder, after completing the projection is cut and 
placed on the flat surface, we'll get the mapping graticule - cylindrical projection. This 
cartographic network was used by the Flemish mathematician and cartographer Mercator. 
  
Croatian Hydrographic Institute (HHI) in Split for construction of the cartographic graticule in 
Mercator projection is basing on the Bessel ellipsoid. For practical navigation, Earth ellipsoid 
can be considered as a sphere.

1.1.2. Types of charts 
 
Charts for sailing are classified according to scale, content, and purpose as: 
➢ Navigation charts  
➢ Information charts and  
➢ Utility charts. 
 
Navigation charts are used for plotting the position of the boat, courses in navigation and 
orientation in navigation. The base for these charts is the Mercator chart. 
 
They are divided according to the scale to: 
➢ General, 
➢ Course, 
➢ Coastal and 
➢ Plans. 
 
General charts show larger areas of the oceans and seas with the corresponding parts of 
the coast and usually are in smaller scale. Mainly used in preparation for navigation works 
of drawing the general route. 
 
Course charts showing parts of individual seas and give all important data for navigation. 
They are used as general charts, as well as for the direct conduct of boats outside the 
immediate coastal area when the safety of navigation permits. 
 
Coastal charts show in detail the smaller parts of the coast (and the sea) and are a 
fundamental navigational tool. Their scale is from 1:50 000 to 1: 100 000. 
Coastal charts are used for immediate orientation, plotting the position of the boat while 
navigating the narrow coastal zone. On them, we can add warnings and dangerous areas 
important for navigation. 
 
Charts - Plans show smaller areas, e.g. port, anchorage, passing, etc. On them, we have 
all important details so they are usually in large scale.

1.1.3. Content of charts 
 
The chart consists of topographic and hydrographic sections.  
 
Topographic part of the chart is yellowish-orange (ocher) color and shows the islands and 
the coastal shore that can be seen from the sea, and on it the characteristic objects for 
recognition of the coast and determining the position of the boat.  
Relief of the topographic part is shown by contour, lines connecting points of the same 
elevation. Coastline corresponds to the high water line. Height is given in meters and that of 
the mean sea level. 
 
Hydrographic part of the chart is partially printed in shades of blue and to depths of up to 
5m in blue, and the depth of 5-10m light blue. Other hydrographic parts are white. Depths 
are marked with isobaths or numbers, and the more we have them, the chart is more 
trustworthy. 
Depths are given in meters, in the title of the chart it is written from what water levels are 
measured (mean sea range of low spring tide usually). 
Type of the seabed is indicated by abbreviations. In addition, we have symbols to accurately 
show the reefs and rocks, the border sector of marine lights, data on currents (direction and 
speed), and important orientation facilities.   
 
Title of the chart is usually on the land area so that it does not disturb the sea area, with 
general information, and the number of tickets in the lower right corner and upper right 
corner. At several places on the chart, we have magnetic or true rose within which we get 
the data on the magnetic declination (variation). 0° of magnetic rose is oriented in the 
direction of the magnetic meridian, and the true rose direction is in the direction of the true 
meridian. The charts published by HHI only true roses are drawn. 
In the lower left corner of the chart is a record of the small corrections based on Notice to 
Mariners. 
 
Chart scale shows how many times a certain length on the chart is reduced of the same 
length in nature. It can be given to the number or ratio of fraction (1: 100 000 or 1/100000). 
 
The frame of the chart consists of the edge and scale of latitude and longitude.  
Latitude scale is also a measure of the distance (1 minute of latitude equals 1 Nautical mile).  
Lengths of individual minutes of latitude scale on the chart expand with increasing latitude, 
and therefore the distance should be measured at the level of the position of the ship (right 
or left). Distances should not be measured on a longitudinal scale (at the bottom or top edge 
of the chart).

Magnetic or true roses are drawn in several places on the chart. They can be used as a 
reference for courses and bearings. Value of the magnetic variation for the area is written in 
the magnetic rose. If there is no room for rose or rose is not practical to be placed, magnetic 
variation for specific areas is given in rectangles on the chart.

Maintenance of the charts - For the safety of navigation it is particularly important 
systematically to warn sailors with all the changes in the navigation area.  
Before using charts, charts should be well studied so that during the navigation more 
attention is given to immediate orientation. We need to check whether the charts are 
corrected to the date of commencement of navigation. 
Publication Notices to Mariners issued by HHI, contains all information relevant to the safety 
of navigation in the Adriatic Sea and in the area of the Ionian Sea and the island of Malta.  
The purpose of the Notices is that during the year we can continuously maintain in the 
current state all the charts, manuals and other publications published by HHI. Instructions 
are given in the NTM on how to use them and how to properly correct charts. They are 
published monthly. NTM can be found on the website HHI (WWW.HHI.HR). 
On the basis of these publications, we can correct any chart to be up-to-date from the date 
of the issue or the latest corrections to the day before sailing out.  
 
The process of correcting charts – From Notice to Mariners take out the content changes 
and establish whether it is permanent or temporary. If the change is temporary, next to the 
number of the respective NTM we have marking T, and for the previous correction letter P. 
Temporary changes are entered with pencil as required by the NTM, and in order to register 
the correction at the nearest edge of the chart, type the number of NTM and year of 
publication. 
Permanent changes are entered in purple or red ink as required by NTM, a record in 
implementing changes is registered in the lower left corner, under the frame in Small 
corrections. Enter the month and year of the NTM. 
 
 
 
Picture - Small corrections 
 
On all charts that HHI issued as the NEW ISSUE markings of the marine data is in English 
(the characteristics of light, color and obscured sectors, colored buoys, holding ground, .....). 
Charts in the new edition repeal all charts from previous editions/circulation. 
  
On the charts that HHI issued as the NEW EDITION markings remain on Croatian. They 
must comply with the data from previous editions to maintain the NTM.

1.1.4. Informative charts 
 
Information chart 101 INFO issued by HHI shows the Adriatic Sea and contains important 
information for safe navigation. Not used for navigation and is not subject to correction by 
NTM, required to have on the boat. 
 
 
Picture - Survey chart Adriatic Sea, Eastern Coast 
Picture - The bathymetric chart of the Adriatic Sea 
 
1.1.5. Utility charts 
 
The utility charts contain different information, depending on the purpose as such, for 
example; radar charts and similar. 
 
 
 
Picture - Utility chart 402 Adriatic Sea, the middle and south section 
 
1.1.6. Electronic charts 
 
The use of electronic charts is growing rapidly in the navigation practice. The reasons are 
varied; archiving and correction is simpler, and in addition, can be used in combination with 
all instruments for navigation; GPS, radar, gyrocompass, echo sounder, etc. 
In some form already present on most ships, usually as plotters that enable accurate 
positioning using a GPS receiver or a radar. 
Using electronic charts at any time we can obtain our true position, we can take out from the 
memory of the plotters data on the past position at any time, we can plan navigation and 
pre-determined points of turn (course change). Higher quality electronic charts display much 
more details and the chart scale is changed by zooming in and out. 
 
Two essentially different types of electronic charts are mainly used:

➢ raster electronic charts and 
➢ vector electronic charts. 
 
Raster electronic charts are electronic reproductions of classical paper charts.  
On the monitor, they look much like regular navigational charts and contains the same 
information. 
Increasing the chart does not increase the number of details, but the only prominent show 
details already mapped. 
 
Vector electronic charts differ significantly from the raster. Only a specific area is shown 
by executing programs that mathematical (vector) analysis graphic prints contours of the 
coast and all the facilities in the area shown on the chart. The appearance of vector charts 
is different from the appearance of raster charts.

Picture - Vector electronic chart

1.2. WATERWAYS AND BUOYAGE, IALA SYSTEM 
 
Fairway is a sea belt sufficiently deep and wide for safe navigation which is, if necessary, 
marked with proper markings. Navigation routes may be: obligatory, recommended and 
prohibited. For technical equipment of the waterways in the Republic of Croatia responsible 
company is Plovput (Split). 
Fee for the use of navigation safety facilities is the fee paid by all shipowner domestic and 
foreign that use the services of Croatian ports. With the money collected from fee 
maintenance of lights is financed, as well as the maintenance of the lighthouses and the 
buoys in the Adriatic. 
Waterways in the coastal sea are marked with special marks and devices. They provide 
seafarer with means of determining the position of a ship in all conditions for navigation or 
safe course in relation to the existing dangers. 
Navigation marks are placed on the important navigational points needed to securely 
approach the coast, sailing in coastal areas and sailing into the harbour. 
The common name of navigational marks is buoyage. 
Navigation marks may be concrete or iron constructions, buoys or floating marks depending 
on the purpose. During the day we distinguish them by shape (cone, pyramid, cylinder, 
sphere, pole vault), forms of the peak of the mark and the color (green, red, black or a 
combination). 
At night we distinguish them according to light signals (clear, red, green). 
Buoyage marks are divided into marine lights and day marks. 
Waterways and dangers to navigation are indicated by the two systems: the lateral (side) 
and cardinal (basic). 
 
Picture - IALA system areas A and B 
 
Marking of waterways is internationally uniform. International Organization IALA 
(International Association of Lighthouse Authorities) adopted a system A and system B. 
Signal characteristics are described in manuals for navigation (Pilots and the list of lights 
and fog signals), and should be examined prior to entering their area.

1.2.1. Buoyage marks (IALA - System A) 
 
Optical marks 
 
➢ Marine lights 
➢ Marks of the lateral system and a modified lateral marks 
➢ Marks of the isolated danger and new dangers 
➢ Marks of the cardinal system 
➢ Marks of safe water and other special marks 
 
Sound and electronic marks 
 
➢ Sound buoy (buoy with a bell, whistle-blowers buoy, the buoy with a gong, underwater 
acoustical means). 
➢ Electronic tags (any radio buoys, beacons, maritime laser light, active and passive radar 
beacons, radar stations and similar) 
 
Information about these resources can be found in the manual for the Radio services. 
 
1.2.1.1. Marine lights 
 
Marine lights are optical signals that have their own source of light and can be placed on 
land, on isolated important points, buoys and lighthouse ships. 
Marine light is divided into guarded and unguarded. 
 
➢ Guarded naval lights are the lighthouses and ship lighthouses. 
 
Lighthouses are the largest and most important objects for navigational safety which 
provide secure day and night navigation of certain sea area. 
They are built on the most prominent and/or the most remote points of the territorial sea. 
They consist of stone buildings with prominent lighthouse tower.  
They are mostly equipped with main and reserve light. The range of the headlamp is up to 
30 nautical miles. Some beacons are fitted with radar reflection device (racon) and/or the 
system for fog (fog detectors and fog sirens). 
All lighthouses are automated and controlled via the remote control. 
 
Ship lighthouses are special ships anchored at the points that are important for navigation.  
Coloured by very distinct colour and the flanks have written a name that can be discerned 
from a distance. 
The purpose of a lighthouse is to reprimand seaman on existing risk and to enable him to 
have good orientation and safe operation of the ship (for positioning), especially when 
approaching the coast from the open sea. 
 
➢ Unguarded marine lights can be coastal, port and lighted buoys. 
 
Coastal light indicates the parts of the coast, passages, channels, harbor access, and 
navigational obstacles.  
They are built on a prominent island or coastal capes, and other important points, mainly 
within internal waters. Coastal lights are orientation points of the second order and are 
intended exclusively for navigation in coastal areas.

Harbour lights are placed on the orientation points of the third order, ie. port and port 
approaches. They are used to ship more easily enter the port, to the anchorage. 
 
Floating buoy lights are buoys with a flashlight. They are placed where we want to mark 
isolated shallows, underwater hazard or safe waterway. Their position may not be accurate, 
so it is not advisable to observe them to determine position. 
 
➢ The characteristic of marine lights 
 
In order to determine certain marine lights, they have different characteristics, which are: 
 
➢ color (and sector-specific color) 
➢ character (light rhythm), 
➢ period, 
➢ elevation - the height of the lights (in meters),  
➢ visibility (in nautical miles). 
 
Color - Colors in the maritime lighting: bijelo (B) or English white (W), crveno (C) or Eng. 
red (R), zeleno (Z) or Eng. green (G) and the orange-yellow. 
The highest visibility has white, then red, and green light. Orange is better seen in the fog. 
 
Sector - In order to mark dangerous or safe for the boating sector, certain marine lights 
except common characteristics have sectors highlighted in different colors. It is not fixed 
what colors is to be used for dangerous or safe sectors. The sector may be unlit, ie. obscured 
or covered. 
 
 
 
Picture - Lights with sectors 
 
Characteristic of the lights - Characteristics of the lights indicates the mode (rhythm) of 
the marine light, but it may be: continuous or alternating (red, white, green), fixed (F), 
flashing light (Fl), have flashes in the group (Fl (#)), the long flashes (L Fl), in quick bursts 
(Q ...), at a very quick bursts (VQ ...), isophase (iso) light and dark same length, etc.

Period of the light is the time interval that elapses from the beginning of one group to the 
beginning of the next group of light signals contained in the rhythm (character) of the lights. 
  
 
 
Picture - Period of marine lights 
 
Altitude (Height of light) - Height of the light is the vertical distance between mean sea 
level and lightbulb (focus) lens lamps expressed in meters. 
Visibility of the light is the distance in nautical miles of that maritime light can be seen from 
a height of 5m of the observer’s eye. 
 
All details of marine lights are given in the navigation manual List of Lights and Fog Signals 
for specific sea area, and basic, on navigation charts. If there is a change in the maritime 
light Notice to Mariners gives corrections. 
 
Corrections published in the form of vouchers are published every month in the Notices to 
Mariners.

The inscription REPEALED or REMOVED is used when the light/signal lights are 
permanently put out of use, and the FOG SIGNAL DOES NOT WORK then when the fog 
signal is temporarily out of use. Corrections should be cut out and stick to the inside edge 
of the page with certain light. Corrections whose value has diminished for any reason will be 
cancelled in Notices to Mariners. These coupons must be removed. 
 
It is more practical in the List of Lights and Fog Signals with a soft pencil to round up the 
number of light if for the light we have changed. 
 
1.2.1.2. Marks for lateral system 
   
Lateral (side) system indicates the left and right sides of the safe waterway or channel which 
a ship has to sail.  
Right or left, sets a course that ship must take upon approaching port or navigable channel. 
The channels that go parallel to the coast, the sides are determined to navigation in the 
north course and towards upstream navigation. 
If the fairway follows the shore, the sides are determined by the direction of navigation 
corresponding to clockwise direction. In the river navigable for maritime vessels sides are 
determined by the upstream run. 
Marks for the left side have a cylinder shape, half-cylinder with a truck or poles with a vertex 
symbol cylinder, RED color. At night their position indicates a red light.  
  
 
 
Picture - Lateral marks, system A 
 
Marks for the right side of the fairway are conical with a pole or poles with a vertex symbol 
cone and are colored GREEN. At night their position indicates the green light.

The modified lateral marks - Bifurcation of the fairway (channel) and an indication of the 
direction of the main (more preferred) channel means is also indicated by lateral markings 
or signs that are colored with red and green horizontal stripes. 
Cylindrical mark has more red stripes, red peak sign and red light (flares in group 2 + 1) and 
indicates that the main channel is to the right of it (the left leaves). 
Conversely, codes conical (cone peak sign) with more green stripes, green peak and a 
green light symbol (flashes 2 + 1) indicate that the main channel is to the left. 
 
 
 
Picture - The modified lateral marks 
 
 
 
 
 
Picture - Identification of the main channel, the main channel to the left

1.2.1.3. Isolated Danger Mark 
 
Isolated Danger Mark indicates that around it, it is free to navigate. The shape is a pole 
and is colored with black and red horizontal stripes. The vertex is a double black ball, and 
can have light; white flashes in two groups - Fl (2). 
 
 
 
Picture - Isolated Danger Mark at sea 
 
 
Picture - Isolated Danger Mark 
Picture - Isolated Danger Mark on the chart 
 
 
New danger mark indicates the newly identified risk that is not tagged in the navigation 
chart, which is not put into the guides for navigation. 
Very important dangers are marked with the two identical markings apart by a few tens of 
meters. 
One of these labels can have RACON radar beacon (responder) with the code signal letter 
“D” on the radar screen with a length of 1 nautical mile.

Picture - Identification of new danger for navigation 
 
1.2.1.4. Marks for cardinal system 
 
Marks of this system indicates safe side (quadrant) to avoid certain danger and the deepest 
water which one should sail past isolated danger. 
The side of the compass cardinal mark indicates is the side necessary to pass around buoy, 
regardless of the direction of navigation. Marks are usually moored buoys or built cylinders 
or poles and are painted in black and yellow horizontal stripes. On top, they have a pair of 
cones, and their respective positions and characteristics of the light depend on the quadrant. 
 
➢ N quadrant - two cones pointing up (short or very short white Fl) 
➢ E quadrant - the base of two cones lie to each other (short or very short Fl (3)) 
➢ S quadrant - two cones pointing down (short or very short Fl (6) separated by longer Fl) 
➢ W quadrant - tips of cones lying to each other (short or very short Fl (9)) 
 
 
 
Picture – Marks for the cardinal system at sea

Picture – Marks for the cardinal system on the chart 
 
 
Picture - Cardinal system marks

1.2.1.5. Safe water mark 
 
Safe water mark means that the surrounding water is safe for navigation, to indicate the 
middle of waterway or channel. A spherical, half roll with a pole or poles and are colored red 
and white vertical stripes. Peak sign is one red ball and can have white light: isophase, 
intermittently, flashes or Morse character A. 
 
 
 
Picture - Safe water mark 
 
1.2.1.6. Other special marks 
 
Other special marks have an informative meaning. They can denote zones of the seabed, 
automatic meteorological and oceanographic buoys, areas for the exercise of the armed 
forces, the positions of submarine cables and pipelines, areas for sports and recreation and 
similar. 
The shape is not fixed. Yellow in color with a peak symbol shaped like letter “X”. They may 
have a yellow light.   
 
 
Picture - Special marks 
 
1.2.1.7. Marks for bridge 
 
 
Picture - Left side of the bridge, the best point of the passage, the right side of the bridge

1.2.1.8. Markings for prohibited anchorage 
 
Marks for prohibited anchorage is inversely drawn anchor black on a white background. In 
channels, passages and rivers mark is set on both sides. On the charts, it is a red crossed 
anchor. 
 
 
Picture - Indication of prohibited anchoring on the chart and on the coast 
 
1.2.1.9. Sound and electronic marks 
 
In the bleak and foggy weather, when visibility is limited for the safety of navigation we have 
sound signals and radio signals or general signal lights. 
 
They are placed at dangerous or navigational important points. Audio signals can be air and 
underwater. 
 
These signals indicate to the seafarer that the ship is approaching the danger and must take 
precautions because it is difficult to determine the direction and distance of sound sources. 
 
In recent times the use of electronic marks is most common because their work does not 
depend on the state of the atmosphere, and the special advantage is a large range, but also 
they increase the safety of navigation in all conditions. The data on these marks for Adriatic 
Sea can be found in the manual Radioservice.

1.3. CHART WORK 
 
1.3.1. Elements of the Earth as a globe 
 
The sphere is a body whose all points of the surface are equidistant from the center. 
Earth's axis is the imaginary line around which the Earth rotates.  
At the ends of the axis, we have geographic poles: northern PN (North) and Southern PS 
(South). 
 
Equator - the largest circle with a plane perpendicular to the Earth's axis and splits Earth 
into the northern and southern hemispheres. 
 
Parallel - a small circle with a plane parallel to the plane of the equator (Equator) and thus 
perpendicular to the earth's axis. 
 
Meridian - the great circle that passes through Earth's poles. Earth poles divide meridian 
into two parts. Meridian passing through the old observatory in Greenwich is called prime or 
zero meridian and divides Earth into eastern and western hemispheres. 
 
 
 
Picture - Elements of the Earth as a globe 
 
Properties of Mercator chart grid are: 
 
➢ The line of equator and parallels are mutually parallel lines, 
➢ Parallels are unequally spaced to each other, between two parallel chart distance 
increases with increased latitude, 
➢ Meridians are parallel to one another, spaced equally in all latitudes, 
➢ The direction of navigation is shown direction, 
➢ Distance can be accurately measured directly on the chart and on the scale of latitude 
in the level of the position of the ship, 
➢ Boat position on the navigation chart is plotted in a rectangular coordinate system (φ, 
λ).

Picture - Mercator projection 
 
1.3.2. Geographical coordinates 
 
The position of the ship or any point on Earth (as sphere) is determined by geographical 
coordinates; latitude (φ) and the longitude (λ). 
Geographical coordinates determine the position of the point with respect to the equator and 
the prime meridian. 
 
Geographical distance from equator or LATITUDE (φ) is the arc on the meridian between 
the equator and the observed points or corresponding angle in the center of the Earth. 
It is calculated in angular units from the equator (00° 00.0') to the north or south pole (90°).  
It is the five-digit number of which the first two digits are degrees, and the other three minutes 
and tenths of a minute, with appropriate marks, the northern or southern hemisphere is 
indicated by the letters N (North) or S (South) behind the numbers of latitude. 
In arithmetic operations, N values are inputted as positive values with '+' sign, and the S 
values as negative, with the symbol '-'. This is internationally decided. 
 
Geographical distance from prime meridian or LONGITUDE (λ) is a shorter arc on the 
equator between the Prime Meridian and meridian of any location or appropriate angle in 
the center of the Earth. 
It is calculated in angular units of the Prime Meridian (000° 00.0') to 180° to the east or west. 
It is a six-digit number. The first three numbers are degrees, and another three minutes and 
ten portions minutes, with appropriate markings E (East) or W (West) that indicate the 
position of Meridian in respect of the prime meridian. 
In arithmetic operations, E values are inputted as positive values with '+' sign, and the W 
values as negative, with the symbol '-'. This is internationally decided. 
 
Degrees are divided into minutes and minutes to seconds. Most often minute is divided into 
tenths of a minute. One minute of latitude equals one nautical mile or 1852m. 
 
1° = 60'; 1' =  60''; 1' latitude = 1Nm; 1/10 Nm = 1 cable;  1'/10 = 60''/10 = 6'' = 1 cable

Picture - Geographical coordinates 
 
1.3.3. The basic navigation tasks 
 
To solve navigation tasks on a chart we need: 
➢ Divider for plotting of geographical coordinates and the distance measurement, 
➢ Navigation triangles or plotter for plotting or reading of courses, and azimuth and 
➢ Pencil and a soft eraser.  
 
 
 
Picture - Navigation set to work on the chart

1.3.3.1. Reading of the geographical coordinates of the position of the boat or some 
points 
 
Set the top of one leg of the compass (one end has marking device) or divider (both ends 
ending in sharp points) in the set point, and the second leg of the divider to the nearest 
parallel making sure that both ends of the divider are parallel to the closest meridian. 
Not changing the spread of the divider, track parallel to the nearest latitude scale (left or 
right on chart) and read the value of latitude in degrees and minutes.  
In the same way, we read longitude value. The top of one leg of the divider is in the set point 
and the second leg of the divider to the nearest meridian. 
Without changing the opening of divider legs bring it to the nearest line of the meridian of 
longitude scale (up or down on chart) and read the value of the longitude of the given point. 
  
 
 
Picture - Reading and entering geographic coordinates using a compass or divider 
 
Important note 
 
The GPS system is based on the ellipsoid WGS 84 and when the position is obtained with 
the GPS and then plotted on the chart made by HHI which uses Bessel, there are notes on 
how much value of the GPS position should be shifted (corrected). 
 
 
 
Picture - Note on the chart published by the HHI relating to the plotting of positions obtained by the GPS 
device

1.3.3.2. Entering the geographic coordinates of the position of the boat or some points 
 
Method 1. - On the nautical triangle that is also protractor we have a line that divides it into 
two equal halves, a line of 90°. Place this line over meridian lines on the chart near the scale 
of latitude (left or right). Another triangle is placed under the first triangle, and then we move 
the first triangle by gliding it up or down until it stands in a level of the necessary latitude. 
 
  
 
 
We observe approximately where our longitude is, bring first triangle parallel to that location 
and at the level of latitude but close to longitude point we draw short (1-2 cm) line. We repeat 
the procedure but now the line of 90° covers the nearest parallel and when the triangle is in 
the level of our longitude we draw a short line across already drawn line to get a cross with 
four quadrants. We draw a circle around the cross center, and if necessary, erase with a 
soft eraser excess horizontal lines. 
 
Method 2. – The sharp point of the compass is set on the mark on the scale of latitude that 
we need to draw, and the other end (with pencil) to the closest parallel. We then transfer 
measured distance from the nearest parallel on to the meridian closest to our longitude (be 
careful up or down). Draw a small indication. 
Then the top of one leg of the compass is set on the mark on the scale of longitude, and the 
other leg in the closest meridian. Transfer this on to the closest parallel and draw small 
indication and also we place sharp end of divider into previous mark drawn on meridian line 
and draw arc in the level of parallel.  
We now have an arc of longitude drawn on the chart. We open divider from nearest parallel 
to the mark on meridian drawn previously and place one sharp end on to the mark drawn on 
parallel to draw arc of latitude. The intersection of two arcs is our position. 
Draw circle around the intersection, erase with a soft eraser marks on the parallels and 
meridians. 
 
Method 3. – This method is similar to the previous one, but the accuracy depends on users 
individually. We just skip the part of drawing small marks or indications for latitude and 
longitude on closest meridian and parallel. This method is not recommended for people with 
no experience in technical drawing and precise usage of compass and divider.

1.3.4. Measuring units in navigation 
 
For the distance measurement and the travelled distance, in maritime navigation, we use 
nautical (sea) mile. 
For nautical mile is adopted a length of 1' of the arc of the great circle of the Earth as a 
sphere or the length of 1' of the meridian arc in mean latitude (45°) as the Earth ellipsoid 
and is rounded to be equal to 1852 m. 
Is divided into 10 parts, 1/10 (one-tenth) are called cable and 1 cable equals 185.2 m. 
The time is measured in hours, minutes and seconds. 
Speed is calculated in knots (kn), by the speedometer. When sailing with speed of 1 knot 
ship travels 1 nautical mile (1852 m) in 1 hour.  
The sea depth and altitudes of the objects on charts in Croatia and publications are given in 
meters. 
 
1.3.4.1. Measuring distances 
 
Open divider legs between two positions or points for which you want to measure distance. 
Then the divider is placed on the scale of latitude in the level of the area of navigation and 
readout value. The number of minutes give nautical miles and tenths of minute’s cables. 
If the distance is too big for the divider, we spread divider into something good for 
multiplication (4, 5, 6 miles etc.) and measure distance in parts, multiply that number and 
add the rest.  
 
 
      
Picture - Measuring distances 
 
1.3.4.2. Plotting the distance 
 
On a scale of latitude measure desired/needed distance with compass and transfer it to the 
desired position/point on the chart. 
 
1.3.4.3. Determination of speed, working on a logarithmic division (nomogram) 
 
For safe maritime navigation, it is necessary to know the distance between the two positions, 
time and speed. If you know two of these, the third can be easily read from the chart if the 
chart has nomogram (logarithmic division), and we could avoid using the equations: 
 
d = s × t         or          d = s × t / 60 (if time is in minutes) 
 
➢ d - the distance travelled (measured in nautical miles - Nm or M) 
➢ s - speed (measured in knots - kt [Nm/h])

➢ t - time (gap - measured in hours - h or minutes - min) 
➢ The procedure when we know distance and speed or distance and time 
If we know distance (d) one leg of the compass is set to value number of distance on 
nomogram and the second leg of the compass to the second set value (speed or time (in 
minutes)). 
Not moving legs of the compass one leg is transferred in 60, and under the other leg is we 
can read time (in minutes) or speed. 
 
➢ The procedure when we know speed and time, and we need distance 
If you need to calculate distance, one leg is placed in the amount of speed or time and one 
in 60. Not moving legs of compass one leg is placed in second set value, and under the 
other leg we read out distance travelled in nautical miles. 
 
 
 
Picture - logarithmic division 
 
1.3.5. Orientation at sea 
 
Navigation at sea is possible in relation to points, planes, and directions in relation to the 
observer. The surface of the sea which the observer sees at the open seas has the shape 
of a circle. The circle which apparently is bounded by the surface where the sky meets the 
sea (apparently) is called the sea horizon. 
The coastal horizon occurs when an island, part of the coast is located within the marine 
horizon. This horizon is called the coastal horizon. 
  
  
 
 
Picture – Division of horizon 
 
Points north (N) and south (S) pole, the East (E) and west (W) defining the cardinal directions 
and split the horizon into the four quadrants. The first quadrant is part of the horizon between 
the north and the east, the second quadrant between the east and south, the third quadrant 
between the south and the west, and the fourth quadrant between the west and the north. 
Division of the cardinal directions are obtained by intercardinal directions: northeast (NE), 
southeast (SE), southwest (SW) and northwest (NW). 
These eight majors traditionally are called winds. The division of the horizon to the wind is 
called wind rose, and its division into degrees a compass rose. 
By dividing the horizon on degrees we can achieve greater accuracy. The entire horizon is 
divided into 360°, and the counting starts from the N point (0°) horizon through E (90°), S 
(180°), W (270°) to N (0° / 360°) clockwise per hour. 
According to that each straight line, or direction in the horizon, has a number of degrees 
from the north (N) point of the horizon.

1.3.6. Basic directions - course, azimuth and bow angle 
 
In the navigation boat leaders must be able to orient themselves in space by direct 
monitoring of objects on the horizon or by using navigational charts. 
Determining direction in which an object is visible is one of the most important duties of a 
skipper. 
In navigation direction is expressed as an angle value of the horizon measured in degrees 
from meridian or fore and aft line of the ship. Depending on from which line angle is 
measured in navigation we differentiate course, azimuth and bow angle. 
The line that divides the vessel into two equal halves is called fore and aft line. The extension 
of the line in the direction of the bow is called bow line or course line. 
The angle between north side of meridian and course line is called ship's course “C”. It is 
measured in degrees clockwise direction from 0° through E (90°) to 360°. The course cannot 
be negative or greater than 360°. 
 
Straight line drawn on a chart from the eye of the observer toward an object is called an 
azimuth line.  
The angle between north side of meridian and azimuth line is called azimuth (bearing) “ω”. 
It is measured in degrees clockwise direction from 0° through E (90°) to 360°. The azimuth 
cannot be negative or greater than 360°. 
 
The angle between course line and azimuth line is called bow angle “L”.  
 
It is measured in degrees from the bow line over to the right side '+' to 180° and to the left 
(-) to 180°.  
Between the course (C), the azimuth (ω) and the bow angle (L) there is a connection. 
 
ω = C + (± L)      + if bow angle is to the right - if bow angle is to the left 
 
 
Picture - Course, azimuth, bow angle and their relationship 
C - course, ω - azimuth, L - bow angle; TN - the true direction; LL - bow angle measured to the left; LR - bow 
angle measured to the right

1.3.6.1. The measurement of courses and azimuths 
 
If the course is measured by navigation triangles on the drawn line of the course we place 
the hypotenuse (longest side of triangle) of one of the navigation triangles, and using the 
second triangle as the bearing first triangle is transmitted until zero of the protractor lies on 
the line of meridian. The angle that the hypotenuse of a triangle is closing with the meridian 
is the ship's course or azimuth, and we read its value from the degrees division on the 
triangle at the point where we see the line of the same meridian.  
 
 
Picture - Measurement of course or azimuth 
 
1.3.6.2. Plotting courses and azimuths 
 
The triangle is placed in meridian closest to the position of the ship so that the zero of the 
protractor lies on the line of meridian. We rotate triangle around the center, so that the same 
meridian is passing through the value of the desired angle of course or azimuth. 
Then the hypotenuse of the triangle is ready to use it as course line or azimuth line. Using 
second triangle hypotenuse is brought to a given position or object and we plot course line 
or azimuth line. 
 
When measuring and plotting courses and azimuth you should pay attention to the 
quadrant of the course and azimuth. 
 
The course is measured and plotted always from the position of the boat in the direction of 
where the boat is sailing, and azimuth from the sea towards the respective object, ie, in the 
direction of viewing.

1.3.7. Earth's magnetism 
 
Planet Earth has its own magnetic field. About the origin of the magnetic field of the Earth 
there are different theories. Earth, like any other magnet has magnetic poles that are 
connected with magnetic field lines. The magnetic field lines, connecting the magnetic poles 
of the Earth, we imagine as magnetic meridians. Magnetic meridians are not great circles, 
but irregular curves. 
Freely suspended magnetic needle will position itself in the direction of the magnetic field 
lines that connect the magnetic poles of the Earth.  
Earth's magnetic pole does not coincide with the geographic (true) poles, and also magnetic 
meridians do not coincide with true meridians. 
The angle between the geographic and magnetic meridian is called variation (magnetic 
declination).  
The variation is stated to be east (E or '+') when the pole of the magnetic needle is shifted 
to the east from the geographic meridian, and west (W or '-') when the pole of the magnetic 
compass needle is shifted westward. 
The position of the magnetic poles is not constant, and the value of variations are variable.  
A variation and its annual change are entered on the navigation chart to a rectangle or in 
the magnetic rose.  
Knowing the variation for the year of publication of the chart and its annual change, we can 
calculate the value of variations in the current year or any required year within 15 years.

1.3.8. Ship magnetism 
 
 
Magnetic compass on land away from the ferromagnetic mass shows the magnetic meridian 
or the direction of the magnetic poles of the Earth. 
On the boat magnetic compass is subject to the influence of ferromagnetic mass of which is 
a boat with equipment made of, and it is subjected to the influence not only the Earth's 
magnetic field but also the magnetic field of the boat. Induced terrestrial magnetism affects 
the magnetic compass system. 
So on the boat northern end of the compass needle is shifted from the magnetic pole to 
compass pole for angle called the deviation of the compass (dev or δ).  
The course, which a magnetic compass is showing on the boats is compass heading (Cc), 
and azimuths read from the same compass are compass azimuths (ωc).  
To get the correct courses and bearings we need to know the value of compass deviation 
at different courses of the boats and add them together with a variation and compass course 
or compass azimuth to get true. 
 
1.3.9. The magnetic compass 
 
The magnetic compass is a device that shows the direction of the magnetic meridian. There 
are different types of the magnetic compass. 
In the boat we need a good compass mounted in the fore and aft or in front of the helmsman 
with the lubber line strictly parallel to the fore and aft.  
Compass comprises of a chamber with a liquid containing the fine needle bearings movable 
in a circle of the compass rose, pointing towards North Pole (N). 
Due to tilting or pitching of the boat compass is filled with fluid that does not freeze at low 
temperatures to prevent oscillations of the needle. 
This gives compass rose certain inertia, it is calmer and so degrees can be read. 
The chamber is closed from above with clear glass, protected from shifting, so that if the 
boat is rolling compass needle still retains a horizontal position. For night sailing rose is 
illuminated with discreet lighting or glow in the dark markings. 
 
 
 
Picture - Magnetic compass 
 
In addition to the built-in ship's compass by which we steer we need to have another small, 
handheld compass for measuring the azimuth. 
 
When measuring compass should be taken to the place that is not particularly exposed to 
the impact of the ship's magnetism, and then measured azimuths are considered magnetic.

Picture - Magnetic and handheld compass 
 
1.3.10. Measuring directions and angles in navigation 
 
Compass is an instrument that identifies meridian. Its use in Europe began in the mid-13th 
century. For a long time magnetic compass was used and only in the twentieth century we 
began to apply other methods to identify meridian. 
Magnetic compasses use Earth's magnetism to set the direction of the meridian.  
Non-magnetic compasses use other natural phenomena to identify meridian, mainly rotation 
of the Earth. Of non-magnetic compass gyrocompass is most often used in maritime 
navigation. 
 
On board during navigation we usually use a magnetic compass. Magnetic compass on the 
land in a certain part of the Earth has a certain error in relation to the direction of the true 
meridian called magnetic variation and is formed under the influence of Earth's magnetic 
field. 
 
As field lines of Earth's magnetic field pass through the ship's metal, the metal parts of the 
ship are causing that magnetic compass has an additional error, the error of deviation. 
 
On nautical charts we can draw and read only the true direction, measured from the true 
meridian. It is therefore necessary to know the value of variation and deviation.  
 
Depending on the meridian in relation to which the course is measured we can have true 
course (CT), magnetic (CM), compass (CC) or gyrocompass course (Cg). 
 
True course (CT) is calculated and measured in relation to the true meridian, or at meridian 
on the nautical chart.  
 
Magnetic course (CM) is calculated in relation to the magnetic meridian, or on the meridian 
which shows magnetic compass on land (away from the influence of ferromagnetic 
materials). Difference between true and magnetic is magnetic variation. 
 
Compass course (CC) is calculated in relation to the compass meridian or meridian which 
magnetic compass is showing mounted on the boat and under the influence of ferromagnetic 
mass of the ship. Difference between true and compass is combined value of variation and 
deviation. 
 
Gyromagnetic course (Cg) is calculated with respect to the meridian that identifies 
gyrocompass.

1.3.12. Compensation of magnetic compass 
 
Compensation is the process of neutralizing the harmful effects of the ship's magnetism to 
a magnetic compass to the boat. Compensation is trying to reduce the value of the deviation 
of the magnetic compass. The process is performed by different methods at separate 
polygons. 
After the completion of the compensation deviation is determined for a minimum of four 
cardinal directions of the compass heading and four intercardinal to create curves and/or 
tables of deviation. 
 
 
   
Picture – Table and curve of deviation of the magnetic compass of the boat 
 
1.3.13. How to check the deviation of the magnetic compass on the boat 
 
As the magnetic properties of the boats are constantly changing, deviation of the magnetic 
compass is not fixed and must be periodically checked. 
 
The simplest way to check the deviation is a by using covered direction. 
 
The covered direction is when we have two distinguished objects covering one another or 
lying on the same azimuth line from us. During our trip, we can spot the best position and 
moment when this is going to occur and at the exact moments when one object is covering 
other we read out compass bearing from our magnetic compass. From the chart, we read 
variation and true bearing by connecting the two objects we observed. The lines that connect 
our position and the two objects at the moment when our two terrestrial objects are at the 
same azimuthal line. At this point, the azimuth between us and the first object is the same 
as the azimuth between the first and second. And, since it is angle measured from chart, 
from true meridian, it is true azimuth. 
The difference between the true azimuth and the compass azimuth is the total error. 
Compass deviation is obtained if the total error is subtracted by variation from the chart for 
our area corrected for the annual change. 
 
TE = ωT - ωC         or            TE = CT - CC                dev = TE - (± var)

1.4. BASICS OF TERRESTRIAL NAVIGATION 
 
Navigation is made up of five basic factors: 1. course, 2. speed, 3. time, 4. distance travelled, 
5. depth. For reliable navigation we should use compass, speedometer, precise watch and 
sonar. 
Depending on the equipment of the boats we use radar and satellite positioning system GPS 
or DGPS. 
Determination of the position of the ship is based on the measurement of angles, distance, 
depth, speed and travelled times, and sometimes position can also be presumed. 
 
For plotting positions on the chart it is necessary to have corrected charts of navigable areas 
and basic equipment: nautical triangles, dividers, compass (divider with pen), pencil, eraser 
and preferably handheld calculator. Safety of navigation depends on the ability of the 
navigator to at all times and in all weather conditions, recognize objects on the coast 
and in their sight, that knows how to orient in space and on the chart and knows the 
basics of determining position. 
Before departure you must carefully study the chart for the navigation area, mark dangerous 
places, areas of shallows and depths less than what you require (plus safety factor), spot 
the distinctive buildings, lighthouses and aids to navigation, and then draw the route the ship 
must follow in safe water, the shortest route from point of departure to point of arrival. 
By knowing the speed of the ship we can have a good estimate of the total duration of the 
trip, and also arrival time at each of the turning points (course alternating points). 
Navigator should be able at all times and in all conditions accurately determine the position 
of the ship.  
In coastal navigation position is determined by observation of characteristic and well-visible 
objects on the coast such as maritime lights, church bells, towers, chimneys, sharply 
prominent peaks of the hills, capes, etc. It is important to identify selected objects and check 
whether they are drawn and marked on the chart. 
Individual observation (measurement) give a line of position (line, curve, and circle) as the 
geometrical point of the ship. 
In the navigation in reach of coast horizon position of the ship can always be determined if 
we have at least two terrestrial line of position.  
Position obtained through intersection of the line of the position is called perceived or true 
position and we mark it by adding circle around the intersection.  
 
Measurements can be simultaneous or in the time span. 
 
➢ Simultaneous observations are those in which the time between two or more 
measurements are very small and practically can be ignored.  
➢ The observations in the time span apply if there is only one object available of which at 
the same time we can only get one line of the position. 
 
We should always strive that ship's position is determined by simultaneous observations 
because such position is more accurate than that obtained in the time span.  
When the position is determined by the simultaneous observation of multiple objects, it 
should be measured as soon as possible in order to reduce time error.

When mariner cannot see shore or identify objects for positioning, one way to get position 
is by calculating travelled distance and plotting it on to the course line.  This is called dead 
reckoning position. Next to this position it is good to log the ship’s speed.  
In coastal sailing boat position is preferably determined every 15 minutes, and before and 
after the course change. During navigation in harsh conditions, or hazardous area it is 
necessary to check the position more often. 
 
1.4.1. Simultaneous observations 
 
Ship's position by observing one object applies when on the horizon only one object is 
visible. Position of the boat is determined by simultaneously measuring the azimuth and 
distance. Distance is regularly measured by radar. Due to the lack of radar on the smaller 
boats this method is rarely used. 
 
Ship’s position by observation of two or more objects is obtained by observing both at 
the same time (or very close). We measure the azimuth of two or more selected objects, 
correct them into true azimuths and plot them on the chart. 
This method requires two, three or more distinguished objects in visible range.  
 
 
 
Picture - Position of the ship with the help of two 
objects 
 
Picture – Position of the ship with the help of three 
objects 
 
1.4.2. The observation of one object in interval of time 
 
Often during sailing we do not have two objects available to read out bearing or distance. 
This often happens in sailing along the coast with little suitable facilities or when approaching 
the coast from the open sea. Especially at night on the horizon we could only see one marine 
light. So we see only one object, but we cannot measure the distance from the object. 
 
In such cases, the ship's position is determined by the two measures of azimuth to the same 
object in the time span. On the course line we mark the distance travelled and then transfer 
first azimuth line parallel to that mark. The intersection of both the transferred first azimuth 
and second azimuth gives us the observed position of the ship for a moment of second 
observation.

Picture - Position the ship's observation of the same object in an interval of time 
 
1.4.3. Dead reckoning and estimated position 
 
Dead reckoning position is determined by the course and the travelled distance in certain 
period of time. From the starting position or from a predetermined position observed at the 
chart we plot line of the true course and mark travelled distance on it (with compass). The 
result is dead reckoning position. Next to it we write speed and time.  
 
If we have a speedometer with indicator of travelled nautical miles we can add that to the 
position mark. 
This method of determining the position is applied when it is the only way of determining the 
position in the open sea at certain intervals. 
 
Dead reckoning and estimated position are least correct positions. There are three main 
sources of errors that influence the accuracy of the position; incorrect steering, imperfections 
of navigational instruments and external influences on the ship. 
 
Because of these errors the intersection of position lines will be triangle, and true or more 
accurate position will be in the middle of the triangle.

1.5. MANUALS FOR NAVIGATION 
 
Nautical chart for technical reasons cannot provide all the necessary information to the 
navigator for safe conduct of the ship, so there are a variety of books and manuals to him 
that help.  
In Croatia navigational manuals are issued only by the Croatian Hydrographic Institute (HHI) 
and Notices to Mariners (NTM) for charts and navigational manuals to be up to date. 
For boat category C it is required to have the following manuals:

1.6. USE OF ELECTRONIC DEVICES ON A BOAT 
 
1.6.1. Radar Navigation System 
 
1.6.1.1. The theoretical basis and the main parts of the radar 
 
Radar (and ARPA, automatic radar plotting aids) is a detection system that uses radio waves 
to determine the range, angle, or velocity of objects. A radar system consists of a transmitter 
producing electromagnetic waves in the radio or microwaves domain, a transmitting 
antenna, a receiving antenna (often the same antenna is used for transmitting and receiving) 
and a receiver and processor to determine properties of the object(s). Radio waves (pulsed 
or continuous) from the transmitter reflect off the object and return to the receiver, giving 
information about the object's bearing and distance, while ARPA can estimate speed and 
direction. 
 
Radar is designed so that on the video receiver directly reads the distance, although he 
actually measures the time. 
 
Marine radars operate at a frequency of: 
a) 9000 MHz (wavelength of 3 cm), the code for these radars X 
These radars provide an image with more details when the weather is nice, have a smaller 
antenna. They are used in short range at which they give an image with more details. 
 
b) 3000 MHz (wavelength 10 cm), the code for this radar is S 
This system has a greater range and they work better in poor weather conditions. 
 
In order for radar to meet the navigation needs it is necessary that: 
➢ it accurately detects objects in a short distance, 
➢ achieve a longer range, or greater detection range, 
➢ able to distinguish objects in azimuth and at a distance, 
➢ can reduce or nullify atmospherics disturbances and  
➢ interference due to reflections from the sea surface. 
 
1.6.1.2. Propagation of radar waves 
 
The antenna emits a radar wave through the directional beam. Radar wave is an 
electromagnetic ultrashort wave, tends to move in a straight line, which allows accurate 
measurements of distances in space. The radar beam spreads through atmosphere at 
increasing space and due to the absorption weakens. Radar waves are affected by weather 
conditions and all physical events in it, and consequences are refraction and reflection of 
radar waves. Since the radar waves spread in a straight line, their range depends mainly 
from the height of the radar antenna, conditions in the atmosphere, and the detection on the 
characteristics of the object. 
 
➢ Refraction is the consequence of the existence of the atmosphere and its physical 
properties. Without this phenomenon distance of radar horizon would be equal to 
geodetic horizon for the respective height of the antenna. In normal conditions spreading 
of radar beam is slightly curved downward, so the radar horizon distance (dra) is about

6% larger than the sea horizon. In order to calculate range in nautical miles we use 
equation dra = 2,22√hant.; where hant represent the height of antenna in meters. 
 
 
Picture - Standard conditions 
Picture - Subrefraction 
 
➢ Subrefraction is a phenomenon that occurs when the layer of cold, moist air above the 
lower thin layer of warm and dry air. This reduces the radar horizon and it is harder to 
detect small object and particularly low objects. 
 
➢ Super Refraction occurs especially in summer when above the wet cold air is warm dry 
layer. The radar beam follows the curvature of the Earth's surface much longer than in 
normal circumstances, so the detection range is larger. More common in coastal areas 
than at the open sea.  
 
 
Picture – Multiple Super Refraction 
 
➢ Multiple super refraction or ducting occurs when the radar beam is first curved towards 
Earth surface and then reflected by the Earth facing upwards, curved downwards and are 
respectively repeated. This phenomenon can lead to the detection of objects at very large 
distances, while objects within the optical horizon will not be detected. 
➢ The influence of the sea surface, or reflection of radar waves from the sea surface may 
enhance or suppress the direct wave of the radar. The result is an increase or decrease 
in range, or echo. 
 
Picture - Reflection 
 
➢ Meteorological conditions or processes in the atmosphere are changing the physical state 
of the atmosphere. Variations in the state of the atmosphere of its standard conditions 
causing the radar beam to abnormally spread and thus causes interfering reflections on

the radar screen, especially in bad weather and storms. It affects the accuracy of the 
information obtained by the radar and reduces the safety of navigation. 
➢ Precipitation causes a reverse echo that at the radar screen appear as blurred reflections 
covering objects in their area. Range of detection of objects is inversely proportional to 
the intensity of precipitation. 
➢ Fog does not affect the distance of detection and on the radar screen does not create 
reflections. 
➢ Clouds have no significant impact. 
➢ Meteorological fronts and tropical cyclones on the radar screen give different reflections 
which vary in form and intensity, thus facilitate avoiding of cyclones. 
 
1.6.1.3. Radar range 
 
 
 
Picture – Maximum radar range 
 
RMAX = 2.2 × (√h1 + √h2) in Nm 
 
Radar range is the greatest distance at which the object can be detected and displayed on 
the radar screen. For navigation it is important to know the height of the radar antenna and 
the physical and geometrical properties of the observed objects. 
 
1.6.1.4. The radar image 
 
Depending on the motion we can have two settings on radar screen: 
 
➢ Relative motion (all objects have sum of our movement and their movement) and 
True motion (all objects have their own true movement displayed on screen). 
 
➢ For example, floating buoy fixed on seabed in relative motion has our counter-course and 
our speed, while in true motion it is an unmovable object on screen. 
 
Picture of radar can be oriented head up, north up and course up. Difference between head 
up and course up is that head up is pointing at where our bow is pointing at, while course is 
pointing in direction of our movement (course). Difference between course and heading is 
combination of wave, wind and sea current influences.  
 
So radar image can be stabilized: 
➢ Over ground – with this setting we have true motion of all objects on radar screen 
➢ Through water – with this setting we have apparent motion of objects under the influences 
listed above. 
 
Anti-collision radar image in oriented towards north, which can automatically resolve 
problems of Collisions at Sea. Automatic Radar Plotting Aid – ARPA is addition to the radar 
that calculates speed and direction and therefor can extrapolate where the object is going 
to be in near future in relation to us and give us warning if there is a chance of collision.

Automatic radar tracking continuously measures and calculates parameters on the basis of 
which position is determined, course and speed of vessels and objects in the area. Number 
of monitored objects depends on the capacity of the computer. The display of the radar 
indicator analyses the situation and we can choose a manoeuvre to avoid a collision. 
 
1.6.1.5. Analysis of the radar image 
 
In order for navigator to be able to analyse the radar image, he/she:  
must know the basics of spreading of radar waves, and the reflective characteristics of radar 
objects, 
➢ must know the technical characteristics of the radar, its possibilities and limitations in the 
existing conditions of navigation,  
➢ must be able to compare the radar image with the navigation chart, and  
➢ must be able to distinguish a true reflection from the false. 
 
Each radar has the ability to show a real vector (real movement of ships) and relative vectors 
(apparently the movement of ships in relation to our direction of travel).

1.6.1.6. Navigation using radar 
 
Radar marks are used to better represent and easily identify objects on the radar screen, 
which is especially important for first radar contact with the shore. There are two types of 
radar beacons: active, so-called RAMARK, and passive, so-called RACON. 
 
Active radar beacon RAMARK is microwave radar transmitter that transmits low power 
radio signals continuously or at specified intervals.  
On the radar screen received signal is seen as a glowing sector radial line passing from the 
center of the screen; based on it we can determined azimuth, and also distance but only if 
the building structure of mark is displayed. 
 
 
Picture - Ramark signal on the radar screen 
  Picture - Racon signal on the radar screen

Passive radar beacon - (responder) RACON is microwave electronic device which 
automatically responds with the special signal received from marine radar stimulating pulse 
corresponding frequency.  
Beacons generally operate at wavelengths of 3 cm. Racon signal beacon on the radar 
screen is shown every two minutes and lasts for about six seconds. It seems like a radial 
solid line or encoded in the form of Morse code, and starts from the parish to the perimeter 
of the screen. 
 
Angular radar reflectors - the auxiliary marks for radar navigation marking the important 
routes, the lines of low and uncharacteristically shore line, shallow waters, underwater 
hazards, poorly visible orientation points and similar.  
They can be fixed on a solid base, e.g. rods, iron construction, masonry codes, etc., or the 
tops of the buoys. There are such buoys the top of which is developed into a separate area 
with corner reflectors. They are placed in and around the lighthouse tower, for cylindrical 
objects resulting in poor radar echo. The corner reflector consists of two or three metal plates 
that are perpendicular to each other. 
 
 
 
Picture - Types of radar corner reflectors 
 
Reflectors are placed on boats and small ships (e.g. yachts, fishing boats), made of wood 
or fiberglass, that could be as reliably detected by radar. Octagonal corner reflector which 
has the length of the sides from 20 to 40 cm is most commonly used. 
 
If the sea is calm, the angle reflectors show on the radar screen at about 4-9 M, depending 
on their shape, material, size and height, the height of the radar antenna, pulse duration and 
weather conditions. 
 
1.6.1.7. Determination of the position of the ship 
 
Navigation by application of radar navigation is based on principles that apply to terrestrial 
navigation. Radar is just one of the supplies that increases the safety of navigation. 
Ship's position is determined by two or more radar line position, similar to the visual 
determination of the position.  
In general, the observations should be carried out simultaneously. It is worth noticing objects 
that give a strong enough echo, which are easily identified and that from the sea can be 
detected from all positions on ship. 
Reliability of radar images largely depends on the conditions of propagation of radar waves 
and technical characteristics of the radar. Therefore, it is necessary to know how to analyse 
the radar image, particularly when approaching the coast from the open sea and in 
navigation at specific conditions.

➢ Measuring the azimuth (bearing) 
 
Bearings and bow angles may be measured by using a mechanical or electronic directional 
finder (cursor) and electronic bearing line (EBL) depending on the type of radar. Radar 
measured azimuths can be kept accurate to ± 2°. 
 
➢ Measuring distances 
 
Distance on radar can be measured by using series of fixed concentric circles whose 
equidistance depends on the scale of the selected measuring range or by usage of variable 
range marker (VRM). 
The accuracy of the distance measured with radar is ± 1% and ± 1.5%; and they are more 
précised than the azimuth. 
 
1.6.1.8. Use of radar in preventing collisions at sea 
 
The invention of radar created new opportunities and benefits for navigation in fog or limited 
visibility, so the radar can be useful for preventing and avoiding collisions at sea.  
However, the radar is just one of navigational aids, so data provided only by the radar are 
not always sufficient for safe navigation. Radar cannot provide all the elements necessary 
to carry out the manoeuvre at short distances. 
In addition it has its technical limitations and sources of error to which we must always count 
on, and that creates some uncertainty to the navigator.  
One of the disadvantages of radar that is on board is that we cannot tell if the other vessel 
sees us or notices us, what are hit intentions, or what manoeuvre he is going to perform.  
Therefore, before we start off with the manoeuvre we should determine course of the other 
boat and the minimum safe distance to pass next to him.  
Experience has shown that the distance of 3 Nm or, in general, the distance twice as large 
required so ship can freely turn by 90°, is the minimum distance at which one can still try to 
avoid a collision.  
Statistics showed that ships usually clash if they face from bow sector to 70° left and right, 
because then meeting is fast and we have very little time to manoeuvre.  
Otherwise, the danger of a collision exists, when the relative movement of the mark on the 
radar screen (indicating the incoming ship) is leading to the center of the screen (our 
position).  
This is generally the case when the bow angle (azimuth) on the other ship remains 
unchanged, or changes very slowly. 
 
Modern radar ARPA systems regularly as part of an integrated navigation electronic system 
enables automatic tracking of ships within the radar horizon and calculation of the necessary 
elements for optimal manoeuvre to avoid collision with another boat. By placing safety 
barriers it allow us to get radar/ARPA alarms if a boat near us exceeds the parameters that 
we set ourselves (minimum closest point of aproach CPA and the time to the closest point 
of aproach TCPA). 
As soon as you begin a manoeuvre to avoid a collision, especially in foggy weather, it is 
advisable to warn about that to other ships (the signals radiotelephone). Manoeuvre is to be 
carried out vigorously, so that other ships can clearly see that we are performing manoeuvre 
to avoid collision.

1.6.2. Speedometer, speed and distance travelled 
 
Accurate knowledge of the speed over ground and speed through the water, and the 
travelled time is one of the essential conditions for safe navigation, especially in poor 
visibility. 
The distance between two places on a chart is called the distance (d in Nm). Part of 
distance that boat travels in period of time is called the distance travelled. Distance travelled 
in an hour is called speed (in knots). 
Speed is measured in knots (kn), and the distance travelled in nautical miles (M or Nm). 
Under suitable conditions of sailing boat speed is proportional to the rotational speed of the 
screw (propeller) and is called the speed through water. Part of path that the boat travels 
at that speed is called the distance travelled through the water. 
When external forces are acting upon boat (wind, waves and current) actual speed of the 
boat is speed over ground and part of the way that the boat travelled at that speed is called 
the distance travelled over ground. 
Speed through the water is measured by an electric speedometer. They measure the 
distance travelled through the water. When there is no effect of the current water speed 
measured by speedometer and speed over ground are the same. Speed over ground and 
distance travelled over ground is determined by determining the position of a ship or 
ultrasonic speedometer.

1.6.3. Echo sounder 
Safety of navigation means and knowledge of the sea depth under the keel boats, especially 
when sailing near the coast and in poor visibility conditions. Measurement of the depth 
allows for better orientation. In addition to the known depth is necessary to use the 
navigation chart with enough marked depth. 
Depths printed on charts can be different from the actual depth because of the tidal 
influences. The sea depth is measured by sonar. 
The most widely used modern echo sounders operate on the basis of the ultrasound passing 
through the water. Special devices for alarm can be connected to the echo sounder. When 
the boat exceeds the set limit of the depth the device will give audio and/or visual warning. 
        
 
 
Picture - Ultrasonic sonar 
 
1.6.4. GPS satellite navigation system 
 
GPS (Global Positioning System) is a system developed for the needs of the armed forces 
of the USA. Today it is a basic satellite radio navigation system. 
 
The principle of operation in the satellite navigation system GPS - Determination of the 
position of the ship is based on the simultaneous very precise measurement of distance 
from artificial satellites orbiting the Earth at a specific orbits. 
 
Distance from the satellite is determined based on the time radio signal travelled from the 
satellite to the ship’s receiver. Therefore, each satellite and receiver must have accurate 
and synchronized clocks. 
 
Distance from one of the navigation satellites corresponds to the radius of an imaginary 
sphere with the satellite in the center. Distance give circle as line of position. 
 
It is necessary to have three to four satellites in range; three are minimum for position 
(intersection of three circles is our position), but with adding fourth data that verifies 
synchronization of clocks (time) we can have accurate position.

Picture – Intersection of three distances 
 
Radio network of GPS system consists of: 
➢ the space part, 
➢ terrestrial supervisory control of the central part and  
➢ user part. 
 
Space part - The system consists of 24 satellites that cover the earth’s surface with radio 
signals in 6 orbits at an altitude of 20200 km. Each satellite has an atomic clock. The 
stabilizing device is constantly directing aerial of satellite towards Earth. 
Satellite orbits lays in the 6 plane inclined to the equator about 55°. 4 satellites are orbiting 
in each orbit. Time for satellites to make one full circle around Earth is 12 hours. Constantly 
above the horizon we have 4 to 8 satellites and to determine the position of the boat we 
require at least 3 to 4 satellites. 
Most preferably satellites in orbits are selected automatically by the receiver. Persistence of 
orbit satellites is the requirement for the accuracy of the position of the ship. Correction of 
the satellites orbit is managed by terrestrial supervisory control center. 
 
Terrestrial supervisory - control central station - it consists of the main station and four 
supporting station. The main station from the associated station receives a coded radio 
messages, and then after processing the transmitted station is transmitting data to them 
once a day to transmitting satellites. 
 
Users of the system - the boats and all other owners of the GPS receiver. 
The satellites transmit radio signals at exactly the precise time. They are transmitting coded 
important messages synchronously on two frequencies, which allows cancelling of 
ionospheric errors. The rapid spread of radio signals is constant; multiplied by the time 
interval gives the distance of GPS device from the satellite. Both frequencies are modulated 
with coded signals. P-code (Code Protected) is the highest accuracy and is designed for 
military needs and exclusively military users of GPS systems can decode it. 
C/A code (Clear Acquisition Code) is intended for general use, the less accuracy. Each 
satellite has its own C/A code by which it is recognized by the ship's GPS receivers. 
 
Differential GPS station - is used for better accuracy of the position of the boat. DGPS 
station consists of the fixed radio receiver on the ground, with the exact geographical 
coordinates. Comparing the exact positions of the radio and the obtained position from the 
satellite is a GPS error or repair. The reference station DGPS correction value is sent via

the radio alerts to the ship's radio receivers. The receiver must be able to receive and 
process the DGPS messages. 
 
On-board GPS receiver and its use - The receiver consists of a cursor with LCD video 
screen, a data processing unit, an electric process computer and memory, and an external 
antenna. Distance from the three to four known satellites corresponding with imaginary 
spheres whose center is on-board receiver. 
 
The LCD screen of the receiver shows the selected navigation data: 
➢ geographical coordinates of the boat’s position, 
course and speed of the boats, 
distance travelled by the boat, 
distance arrival at certain points of the programmed navigation routes,  
date, the current hour, minutes and seconds (UTC and the time zone). 
 
It can store and display the geographic coordinates of individual positions. Almost all modern 
receivers GPS have possibility to connect with certain navigation units. During navigation 
on the video screen of the receiver it displays an image of chart for navigational area on 
which it automatically plots position of the boat.

1.6.5. Automatic steering (auto-pilot) 
 
Automatically acting on the steering wheel, this device in conjunction with a compass does 
not allow the ship to uncontrollably change course. Modern electronic autopilot provides
