Autopilot is regularly integral part of the navigation integrated bridge.

1.7. PLANNING OF MARITIME VOYAGE 
1.7.1. Preparation and selection of the waterway 
 
Navigational safety of boats depends not only on qualifications of the navigator, functionality 
of the navigation equipment, but also a thorough navigation preparation of the boat. 
 
Navigation preparation includes: 
 
➢ study of the area of navigation, 
➢ choice of routes, 
➢ plotting courses 
➢ determining the distance of voyage, 
➢ time od departure and arrival and 
➢ speed choice. 
 
1.7.2. The study of the area of navigation 
 
It is necessary to study the charts and manuals in order to choose the best navigational 
route. Best route depends on hydro-meteorological conditions and equipment of the coast 
and boat navigation devices, the recommendations that are given in guides, traffic density, 
and specific port of arrival. 
 
Plotting - Check that the charts are corrected and up to date. Courses are drawn by the 
shortest and safest way. Along the line exchange data are entered: a true course (CT), a 
compass course (CC), the distance (d) and the speed (s). 
Courses are drawn at a sufficient distance from all dangers, taking into account the space 
needed to manoeuvre the boat to avoid a collision with other ships. 
Courses should be drawn at a safe distance from the coast, which enables recognition of 
objects and marine lights for orientation and determining the position of the boat. 
With regard to the draft of the boat it is necessary to avoid the shallows. Keep well clear 
from the buoys particularly if their position is insecure or at night, when visibility is reduced 
or limited.  
 
The starting position and turning points - The starting position can be a position of the 
anchorage or other positions. Waypoint is chosen so that the boat is at right angle to an 
object (at 90°) or so that the bow angle is as close to 90°. If there are no objects for 
determining point of turn then track position by using dead reckoning. 
 
Distance of travel - The total distance is lenght that the boat must cross and equals to sum 
of all the distances in each course changes. 
 
Speed choice - Selects the best and safest speed for sailing.

1.7.3. The implementation of travel and control the implementation of travel plans 
 
Just before departure it is necessary to prepare the navigation equipment. Boat is brought 
to the initial position, and sets at the initial course. Boat leader or member of the crew in the 
team of the navigational watch operates the boat by following courses plotted on a chart. 
Be sure to monitor the work of all navigation devices, follow the coastline and recognizable 
objects to coast to determine the position of the boat. You can get closer to the coast only 
when you have at least one accurate true position of the ship.  
Safe navigation means the ability of a skipper to recognise coast and assess boat positions 
by orientation to the surrounding objects, tracking the boat on the chart and to the knowledge 
of navigation opportunities related to navigation.  
 
These abilities should be acquired and improved in all weather conditions. Electronic 
navigation systems contribute to the safety of navigation. 
 
During the trip it is necessary to determine the position of the boat before entering in to the 
dangerous area of navigation, before and after the change of course. Based on the travelled 
distance and time check the speed of the boat. 
 
Check the deviation of a magnetic compass. Midday position is indicated on chart with 
special mark, and next to it write date.  
 
At each position and turning points on a chart record time, and in the assumed position 
status of the speedometer. 
 
Receive weather reports and monitor weather conditions. 
 
The sail can be into the port or at anchor. If you are sailing into the harbor study legislation 
relating to the respective port. And if you are coming to the anchorage point examine the 
elements that ensure a safe stay at the anchorage. Make sure not to anchor on forbidden 
places, like the channels, the entrance to port, the area of submarine cables and pipelines. 
These places are marked on the chart with red crossed anchor, and on coast with reverse 
anchor.

2. BASIC KNOWLEDGE OF BOAT ENGINES 
 
2.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
The most common marine engines used on board are combustion engines. Internal 
combustion engines (diesel and gasoline) are heat machines in which thermal energy of the 
fuel, that the combustion inside the cylinder, is converted into mechanical energy in that the 
expansion work of combustion products is transferred by the reciprocating piston and a 
crankshaft mechanism. 
 
Given a theoretical process that takes place in the working cylinder internal combustion 
engines can be divided into: 
Engines with Otto cycle - they intake the fuel mixture and the mixture is ignited with electric 
spark. Combustion happens at a constant volume. 
Diesel engines – they intake the clean air, injected fuel is ignited by means of compressed 
air temperature. A combustion engine works at a constant pressure. 
Engines with mixed combustion in which the combustion takes place partly at constant 
volume and partly at constant pressure - Diesel engines with direct fuel injection (today's 
engines). 
 
As per numbers of strokes in the cylinder, after which the working process is repeated 
engines are divided into: 
stroke (2-stroke) 
four-stroke (4-stroke )

2.4. PRINCIPLE OF 4-STROKE DIESEL ENGINES 
 
Unlike petrol engines, four-stroke diesel engine draws in fresh air (no fuel mixture) or air 
during suction obtained by a pre-pressure. Injected fuel is ignited by means of air 
temperature. At four-stroke engine cycle that is required for the working stroke, is performed 
during two full revolutions the shaft shank, ie. for four stroke or four stroke blends.

2.5. PREPARING THE FUEL MIXTURE WITH OTTO ENGINE 
 
Liquid fuel must be brought into the cylinder of the gasoline engines in small particles. 
Apparatus for spraying fuel are called injectors. To achieve a favourable work of the otto 
engine it is important to maintain a constant ratio of fuel and air (in newer engines this is 
handled by electronics that through sensor optimizes the mixture). Fuel air mixture burns as 
the spark jumps from the spark plug electrodes. To obtain the required high voltage between

2.6. PREPARING THE FUEL MIXTURE WITH DIESEL ENGINE 
 
There are two basic systems of delivery of fuel into the cylinder engine with diesel engine: 
direct injection - compressed air in the piston movement upwards so much that the heat 
ignite the fuel when injected. The air rushing around the cylinders supporting the fuel burns 
rapidly as it manifests itself as a diesel knocking (kicks). 
indirect injection - a pre-combustion chamber, the piston forces the air through the spout, 
causing friction heating the air in the chamber then vortexed. When the fuel is injected and 
ignited by flaming mixture returns through the spout and stirred with compressed air in the 
cylinder. Heat in the neck is then transferred to the next filling of the air. Whirling is very 
great and gives lower level of diesel knocking.

2.8. LUBRICATION SYSTEM FOR MARINE ENGINES 
 
Modern engines are composed of hundreds of moving parts that can bear heavy loads and 
operate at high speed for a long time, and their reliable operation depends entirely on the 
good lubricants - a complex mixture of ingredients that make up a modern engine oil. 
The term lubrication combines all methods of lubrication and actions using the engine oil 
to decrease friction and wear, or other form of material destruction. An essential requirement 
is to keep contact surfaces spaced apart by injecting an amount of lubricant to each other 
and thus preventing shearing of the surfaces.

2.12. LOADING FUEL IN TO THE SHIP, TREATMENT AS PER SOLAS 
CONVENTION 
 
General: 
• It is necessary to know the volume of tanks on your ship. 
• Properly moor your boat on to the coast. 
• Do not moor to another boat and do not tie another boat for yours. Do not mistake 
the fuel nozzle with the rod holder or the water tank opening. 
 
Before refuelling: 
• Make sure that the ventilation is ON in the engine compartment. 
• Make sure that you have an adequate fire extinguisher, that it is functional and that 
you are familiar with its use. 
• Get acquainted with the location of equipment for pollution prevention. 
• Shut off boat engines, lights, gas stove. Disconnect any devices that may cause a 
spark and then turn off the main battery switch. 
• All passengers must leave the boat. 
• Close all doors and windows. 
 
Note for ships with internal petrol engines 
 
Inner gasoline engines pose a high risk of light flammable gasoline. Particular attention 
should be paid to the correctness and maintenance of rubber fuel guns. 
Gas vapours can accumulate in the engine compartment (they are heavier than air and 
gather in the bottom of the space) and if a spark is formed, an explosion may occur.

To reduce the possibility of explosion: 
 
• Do not smoke during refuelling. 
• Regularly inspect the fuel intake hoses, especially the connections. 
• Before starting the engine, ventilate the machine room as long as there is no smell of 
gasoline. 
• Make sure that there is no potential source of sparking in the lower compartments of 
the machine. Pumps which are inadequately connected may also cause sparking. 
• It is recommended that all electrical systems in the engine room with petrol engines 
must be designed to avoid sparking. 
• It would be useful to have a gas sensor in the engine room. 
 
During refuelling 
 
• During refuelling, the nozzle must be in constant contact with the throat so that no 
spark is generated due to static electricity. 
• Fill the tank without locking the nozzle and do not leave the boat. 
• Do not overfill the tank and keep in mind that fuel increases its volume with the 
increase of the temperature. 
• Transferable tanks refuel at the shore. 
 
After charging 
 
• If fuel leakage occurs, pick up the fuel and place the soaked cloths in the appropriate 
container on the land 
• Turn on the engine after you have realized that the engine space is sufficiently 
ventilated and there is no odour of fuel. 
• After starting the engine allow your passengers to board the boat again. 
 
 
The ship which is supplied with fuel in the port shall in a visible place during the day display 
a red flag, and at night put a red light visible from all sides, and keep closed all the hatches. 
Crew and extinguishing agents must be in standby mode. The supply ship or boat fuel 
sources cannot begin before the disembarking of the passengers.

prescribed by the International Convention for the protection of human life at sea (SOLAS 
Convention), the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships Protocol

3. KNOWLEDGE OF THE NATIONAL MARITIME LAW 
 
3.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
The provisions of the Maritime Code determine the maritime and submarine areas of the 
Republic of Croatia and the legal relationship between them, the safety of navigation in 
internal waters and territorial sea of the Croatia, protection and conservation of natural 
marine resources and the marine environment, basic substantive concerns regarding 
vessels, contractual and other relations relating to vessels, registration of vessels, limitation 
of ship owner’s liability, enforcement and insurance on ships. 
 
If the Code does not provide otherwise, its provisions shall apply to all vessels in internal 
waters and territorial sea of the Croatia. 
 
If the Code provides otherwise, its provisions relating to vessels also apply to yachts. 
 
The sovereignty of the Croatia extends to the internal waters and territorial sea of the 
Republic of Croatia, in the air space above them, and on the seabed and subsoil of these 
marine spaces. 
 
In its economic zone and continental shelf of the Republic of Croatia, RC has sovereign 
rights and jurisdiction for the sake of research, exploitation, protection, conservation and 
improvement of natural marine resources, including resources in the seabed and subsoil 
and to perform other economic activities. 
 
Internal waters for Croatia include: 
1) harbours and bays on the coast of the mainland and the island, 
2) parts of the sea between the land area at mean spring low tide of a straight baseline 
for measuring the width of the territorial sea. 
 
Territorial sea for the Croatia include marine belt wide 12 nautical miles, starting from the 
baseline of internal waters towards economic zone.  
 
The starting line consists of: 
1) line of low water along the coasts of the mainland and the island, 
2) straight lines that enclose entering ports or bays, 
3) straight lines connecting points on the coast of the mainland and on the coast of the 
island 
 
The outer limits of the territorial sea is the line where each point is 12 nautical miles from 
the nearest point of the baseline. 
 
All foreign vessels have the right of safe passage through the territorial sea of the Republic 
of Croatia.

Definitions: 
 
Boat is a vessel designed to sail other than a ship or yacht, with the length of 2.5 meters, 
but under 15m, or total power and propulsion gear is greater than 5 kW, cannot carry more 
than 12 passengers. 
 
The term does not include: 
 
- vessels belonging to the other of vessels for the collection, or of carrying out the rescue, 
- craft intended solely for competition, 
- canoes, kayaks, gondolas and pedalling boats, 
- windsurfing boards and boards for surfing. 
 
Yacht is a vessel intended for sport, recreation and leisure longer than 15 meters, 
regardless of whether it is used for personal or commercial purposes, with the purpose of 
an extended stay at sea, cannot carry more than 12 passengers. 
 
Ship except a warship is a vessel designed to sail the sea, with the length over 15 meters, 
or is authorized to carry more than 12 passengers. 
 
Passenger means any person on a maritime facility, except for children under one year of 
age, and persons employed on board in any capacity. 
 
3.2. SAILING AREAS 
 
Boat or yacht depending on its size, design and other technical characteristics can navigate 
to the following areas of navigation: 
 
• Area I - covers international navigation of all seas and waters accessible from the 
sea 
• Area of navigation Ia - covers international navigation to all seas and waters 
accessible from the sea, with a restriction of not more than 20 Nm from the nearest 
land. 
• Area II - covers international navigation on the Adriatic Sea 
• Area of navigation IIa - includes international navigation on the Adriatic Sea, with a 
restriction of not more than 12 Nm from the nearest land. 
• Area of navigation III - includes navigation in inland waters, territorial waters of the 
Republic of Croatia and waters accessible from the sea. 
• Area of navigation IV - includes navigation on ports, bays, mouths of rivers, Croatian 
lakes, and rivers to the borders to which they are navigable from the sea. 
 
In the area of navigation III. we have following limitations: 
• IIIa - 6 Nm from mainland or islands; 
• IIIb - to 3 Nm from mainland or islands; 
• IIIc - up to 1 Nm from mainland and island coast.

3.3. NAVIGATION 
 
The commander of the ship, crew members, a person operating a boat or yacht when vessel 
is underway must apply the prescribed rules of navigation, protection of the sea from 
pollution, and follow the prescribed signals and codes in accordance with the provisions of 
the Maritime Code and regulations adopted in the Maritime Code, which regulates the safe 
and orderly navigation. 
 
In the channels and straits vessels less than 20 meters of length have to keep clear from 
larger ships, but if they want to cross the channel they must do so by the shortest route, ie. 
Perpendicular (90°) to the direction of movement in the canal or straits. 
 
Ships leaving the port have priority over those entering the harbour. 
 
Ships, yachts, boats and seaplanes shall not, when navigating inland waters and territorial 
seas of the Republic of Croatia, approach the coast, namely: 
- ships and seaplanes, less than 300 m away; 
- yachts with a distance of less than 150 m; 
- motor boats and sailboats less than 50 m away. 
 
All boats without mechanical impulse are allowed to sail indefinitely within 50 m of the coast. 
Yachts and boats must sail at a distance of more than 50 m from the fence of the landscaped 
beach, or 150 m from the coast of the natural beach. 
 
In the area of 150 m from the shore, all vessels shall sail with special care, at a speed not 
exceeding 5 knots, and from 150 m to 300 m from the shore, all vessels shall sail with special 
care at a speed not exceeding 8 knots. 
 
Motor boats and jet boats (scooters, jet skis, airbags, etc.) can sail with hydrodynamic 
buoyancy, or glide only at a distance of more than 300 meters from the shore and in the 
area where they are not forbidden to sail with hydrodynamic by buoyancy. 
In the water area from the nautical anchorage to the coast, and at a distance of up to 150 m 
from the nautical anchorage in the direction of the open sea, it is forbidden to anchor 
maritime objects and seaplanes with its own anchorage system, except in case of force 
majeure or distress at sea. 
 
All vessels must be kept at a distance of at least 100m from the diving buoy.

5. STABILITY OF BOATS 
 
 
Picture - The foundation stability boats 
 
The body floats in water if its density is lower than the density of water. 
If the specific gravity of the body is greater than the specific density of water, then the floating 
body with its shape must, before it is completely submerged, push out an amount of water 
that is equal to the mass of the body. The amount of water in m3 multiplied by the density of 
the sea (δ) and the acceleration of gravity (g) gives the weight of the boat (ship) and it is 
called the force of displacement (D) or displacement. 
 
Weight of displacement for ship floating on the sea is: D = V × δ × g 
 
Since the water and boat are in the same gravity field we can canceled out gravity from the 
equastion.  
 
Archimedes' principle: The force of buoyancy is equal to the weight of the displaced 
fluid. 
 
 D = displacement (t) 
 V = displacement volume (m3) 
  δ(Ro) = water density > 1.0 t / m3; Sea > 1.025 t / m3 
 
Only warships are compared with each other according to the displacement. Merchant ships 
and boats are compared with each other according to spatial content (volume) and to the 
deadweight. Determination of deadweight and measures of the boat (ship) is called 
calibration. 
 
Deadweight tonnage is a measure of how much weight a ship can carry, not its weight, 
empty or in any degree of load. DWT is the sum of the weights of cargo, fuel, fresh water, 
ballast water, provisions, passengers, and crew. 
 
Deadweight = D - D lightship (t)

The payload is the portion of the total capacity of boat, from which revenue is derived; 
passengers and cargo. 
Boat for commercial purposes shall not carry more people and more cargo than permitted 
within payload capacity, ie. the boat (ship) should not plunge over the load water line marked 
on the flanks (if any). 
Every person in calculation has 75kg (two children up to 10 years of age count as 1 adult). 
On the boat for transportation of passengers in a visible place must be indication of the 
maximum number of passengers that can be transported. 
 
Ship stability is a ability of the vessel to returned back to the original position after force 
that has moved him from original position, has stopped. 
  
We distinguish between the transverse, longitudinal, dynamic, static stability, the stability of 
the intact condition and damaged condition. 
 
Points whose mutual position affects the stability are: 
B = center of buoyancy 
G = center of gravity 
M = metacentar 
K = keel 
 
 
 
Picture - Basic Ship in point stability 
 
Centre of gravity and centre of buoyancy must be perpendicular to each other, and their 
connecting line perpendicular to the water line. The focus of the force of the weight must be 
located below the metacentre for boat to be positively stable. 
 
MG (GM) > 0 positively stable boat 
MG (GM) < 0 negatively stable boat 
MG (GM) = 0 indifferent vessel 
 
The position of the centre of gravity and centre of buoyancy changes due to: 
- heel or listing 
- cargo loading/unloading 
- change of trim

The centre of buoyancy is the point where we imagine force of buoyancy is effecting the 
boat. 
The centre of gravity is the point for which we image all the weights on board are 
concentrated. Shape of the ship and distribution of weight affects on the position of centre 
of gravity. When the centre of gravity is lower, ship is more stable. 
 
For every vessel curve of statical stability can be calculated or drawn (GZ curve). GZ curve 
is telling us what value of the righting moment will ship have and still be positively stable and 
when he will be turned upside-down and be negatively stable. We read these information’s 
from Stability booklet issued by shipyard.  
Weights on ship affects its stability. Heavier ship has more righting moments and it is more 
difficult to capsize. When the ship is rolling, righting moment enlarges and reaches its 
maximum (peak of the curve). Righting moment (RM) reaches a maximum around 60° of 
inclination. If the ship is tilted more RM is reduced, stability is reduced and the boat can then 
be overturned/capsized. 
 
 
Picture - GZ curve 
 
GZ curve tells us when is the moment of greatest positive stability, when gradually weakens 
or at any angle completely disappeared. Also we can see the stability of the ship in the 
inverted state. 
 
In determining the stability of the boat, boat is loaded with equipment and crew, then: 
- 
Boat with deck or the superstructure that can be sealed is loaded along one side of 
the boat with the weight corresponding to the weight of the four persons per square 
meter, but not more than the allowed number of persons that it can transport; the boat 
is considered stable if the peripheral line of the deck is not at sea level. 
- 
boat without deck or with a partial deck is loaded along the side of the weight 
corresponding to half of the number of people that it can be transport; boat is stable 
if the highest point of the solid side of the boat remains above sea level to at least a 
height in cm as the length of the water line of the boat. 
 
Stability for boats for economic purposes and fishing boats is determined by calculations 
approved by HRB according to the rules of construction for boats.

6. PROVIDING FIRST AID 
 
6.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
First aid is an emergency procedure that is performed on a patient or casualty before 
providing expert medical care and treatment. By providing first aid we prevent death or 
further injuries to casualty, prevent shock and reduce or eliminate pain. Bleeding and/or 
cessation of breathing require immediate assistance to casualty. All members of the ship's 
crew should be trained in first aid. 
When providing first aid we need to make sure our own safety is not jeopardized and then 
check for serious injuries such as: 
- 
Unconsciousness 
- 
Possible internal bleeding 
- 
Pressure or stab wounds 
- 
Bone fractures 
- 
Injuries to the eyes 
Before providing the first aid it is necessary to remove casualty from the unsafe area to 
prevent further injury. 
In the event of multiple casualties call for help and then provide first aid to one at a time 
according to severity of the injuries - until backup arrives.  
 
 
6.2. GENERAL PRINCIPLES FOR PROVIDING FIRST AID ON BOARD 
 
 
 
Picture - Steps of the First aid 
 
• Establish heart action (compression), check airways and establish breathing  
• Check if casualty is bleeding 
• Remove toxic substances 
• Prevent further injury 
• Remove casualty from the room where there is smoke and toxic gases.

In case of accidents, fainting is a common phenomenon. There is a great danger of 
suffocation due to easing of the muscle tone throat and tongue falls back and due to the 
content of the oral cavity. Therefore, unconscious casualty should be set up in the recovery 
position. 
 
 
Picture - Recovery position 
 
 
The process is performed by pulling the wrist and knee on one side of the body. In this way 
we prevent choking and allow free flow of the liquid contents from the mouth, pharynx, 
esophagus and stomach and drain of liquids in to them. Before checking is the casualty 
breathing it is necessary to check the mouth of possible content. 
To check the existence of heart action we can lean our ear on the left half of the front wall 
of the chest. 
Another method is to place two fingers between the bone and the tendon over radial artery 
- which is located on the thumb side of the wrist. Even better way is to check on the carotid 
artery. 
Check for breathing and cardiac activity should be performed as soon as possible and, if 
necessary, start artificial respiration and external cardiac massage. The method depends 
on the casualty breathing and cardiac function.

6.3. CPR AND CHEST COMPRESSIONS 
 
 
 
 
 
 
URGENT – place casualty on a hard, flat surface 
 
Step A - raise the neck, pressing his forehead to 
the back, remove the contents from the mouth 
 
Step B - before beginning CPR check pulse and 
keep checking it every 5min. Blow air into the 
lungs 12 times a minute (through the mouth or 
nose).  
 
Step C – if pulse is missing begin with chest 
compressions. Determine the place of pressing 
and dent sternum 4-5 cm, 60-120 times per 
minute.  
 
If the procedure is carried out by one person 
perform 15 heart pressures and two air 
insufflation; if two people 5 pressures heart on one 
blowing. 
 
Once started CPR must not be interrupted.

6.4. WOUNDS 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Reveal injured body part. If necessary, tear up the 
clothes or the cut with scissors. Clean the dirt from the 
wound with sterile forceps (do not clear of blood clots; 
do not rinse it with water). Use antiseptic on sterile 
cotton or gauze and clean area around the wound in 
the direction of the wound. 
If the wound is still bleeding, stop the bleeding by 
compression bandages. Cover wound with sterile pad 
bandage or more layers of at least 2 cm sterilizing the 
wound environment. 
Attach bend or triangular scarf. 
 
 
Picture - Acupressure points to stop bleeding 
 
The human body contains an average of 5 litres of blood. A healthy person can lose around 
1 litre of blood without serious consequences. More than that is life-threatening. Bleeding 
can be external and internal. External bleeding is when the skin (largest organ) is injured; 
internal bleeding is caused by injury to the blood vessels inside the body, and can be arterial, 
venous and capillary. It can be visible and invisible.

6.5. HEAT WOUNDS 
 
If clothing on person catches fire: 
STOP where you are - moving or running feeds air to the flames and worsens the fire. 
DROP to the floor - if you stand up, the fire can burn your face.  
ROLL slowly on the floor or ground, in a rug or blanket if you can. 
COOL off as soon as possible with water for first and second degree burns. 
Depending on the depth of injury to body burns are divided into four stages of mild to severe. 
Burns affecting more than 18% of the body surface of an adult (10% - children) are life 
threatening. 
Extinguish all the flames before giving first aid. If casualty was quenched with water, remove 
clothing from the casualty. Set casualty on the ground to avoid the shock. Glued pieces of 
clothing do not remove from the body. If casualty is conscious, give abundant liquid, e.g. 
one litter of salted water (water with a spoon of salt). 
 
6.6. SUNSTROKE/HEATSTROKE 
 
A longer stay in bright sunlight can cause life-threatening changes in the brain. The signs of 
sunburn: 
- 
High body temperature, 
- 
Severe headache, nausea, dizziness, and in extreme cases unconsciousness and 
coma. 
First aid for sunstroke: 
- 
Cool of the head area as soon as possible, put cold compresses or ice on head 
- 
Move casualty into the shade 
- 
Lay casualty in bed with a slightly raised headboard and take off clothes 
- 
Provide cold drinks 
- 
Give a means for lowering the temperature. 
 
Burns of first degree cool of by placing under stream of cold water, or dip in cold water or 
sea. The skin around the burns should be cleaned with clean water and soap or antiseptic 
solution. Do not rinse and clean burned surfaces. It can be sprayed with antibiotic spray. 
Burned area needs to be covered with sterile, oily, vaseline gauze. Do not penetrate blisters. 
Burns of second degree and greater are open wounds so it is advisable to put a cloth mask 
for mouth and nose and sterile gloves before providing first aid. 
 
6.7. INJURIES CAUSED BY LOW TEMPERATURES 
 
We divide them in general (Hypothermia) and localized or limited frostbite. A decrease in 
body temperature below 25°C becomes life threatening. The first signs of hypothermia are 
feeling cold, shaking of the muscles, shallow and slow breathing, slow heart rate, decreased 
metabolism. Further lowering of body temperature causes a feeling of fatigue and confusion 
indifference. This is followed by unconsciousness, cessation of breathing and heartbeat. 
Best treatment is to immediately warm up casualty in a hot bath at 42° C or wrapping in 
warm blankets or body to body heat transfer. If casualty is conscious, give hot drinks, do not 
give alcohol. 
Heart and the most distant parts of the body such as the feet, hands, ears, nose and chin 
are most often at risk. Depending on the depth of damage, there are three degrees of 
frostbite. Frozen body parts are hard, grey-white and insensitive. After the frozen part of the

body is warmed up in first stage, the redness and swelling of the skin appears, and in the 
second stage bubbles or blisters. Heating up the frozen part of the body is very painful. In 
the third degree, frozen body cannot be warmed up and it stays frozen and stiff, but after a 
few days it darkens. Frozen body parts need to be heated up gradually and cautiously (first 
place it in cold water then warmer water). Do not rub the frozen part because it can damage 
the skin and pave the way for infection. 
 
6.8. ELECTRICAL HAZARDS 
 
Electricity voltage greater than 50V is dangerous to humans. Harmful effects are twofold: 
the point of contact we have burns, and in the contact with the body electricity spreads 
through the nerves and blood vessels throughout your body causing paralysis of the nerves 
and muscles. 
Casualty should be excluded from the electric circuit, observing your own safety. 
If casualty is not breathing immediately begin artificial respiration (CPR). 
If the heart rate cannot be detected start chest compressions. 
 
6.9. FRACTURES 
 
Bone fractures are one of the most common injuries that require prompt and adequate first 
aid. Due to a muscle, strain bones are usually moved from its place. There are also 
incomplete fractures, and sometimes bones may be broken. We distinguish between open 
and closed fracture. 
Fractures may have side effects such as shock, injury of blood vessels and nerves, and fat 
embolism (blockage of blood circulation pieces of bone marrow). 
 
If you suspect fracture, it should be treated as if it were a proven fracture. 
 
Evaluate the location for risk and safety, and check the location of the fracture, how did it 
happened and size of the fracture, and gently and carefully treat fracture parts of the body. 
Stop the bleeding in previously described ways. In case of open fractures after stopping the 
bleeding process and clean environment around the wound. 
 
 
Broken limb should be hold below and above the fracture point, best in joint above and below 
the fracture, and gently pull in opposite directions. Use splints to immobilize the bone. 
 
6.10. RESCUE OF DROWNING VICTIM 
 
→ Do not overestimate your own capabilities. 
→ Toss a drowning casualty a rope, raft, floating object, vest, and add a boat hook. 
→ Do not jump into the sea head first and use a life jacket. 
→ Remove a drowning casualty as soon as possible from the water. 
→ Proceed according to the methods for reviving (CPR)!

7. METEOROLOGY AND OCEANOGRAPHY 
 
7.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
Heat is a form of energy. Thermal energy is not standing still but moving. It spreading from 
a warmer to colder things, until their temperature is equalized. Heat energy moves in three 
ways: by conduction, convection and radiation. 
The temperature is measured with the thermometer, and expressed in degrees Celsius or 
Kelvin (°C); while thermal energy is expressed in joules (J). 
 
The force with which the column of air is pressing against the surface of 1cm2 is called 
atmospheric pressure (AT) and is expressed in hectopascals (hPa) and the millibar (mbar). 
 
Atmospheric pressure is different at different locations. Depends on the temperature and air 
density, and hence on the altitude, and the amount of water vapor and air flow. 
 
The atmospheric pressure of 1013 hPa at 15° C temperature at 45° latitude, and at the sea 
level is called the standard atmospheric pressure (1013.25 mbar; 760mmHg). 
 
The lines on synoptic chart that connects places with the same atmospheric pressure are 
called isobars. The importance of the isobars on a weather chart is that if the isobars are 
closer together (denser) the wind is stronger, and the movement of a particle of air follows 
the line of isobars. 
 
 
 
Picture - Isobars on a weather/synoptic chart 
 
Atmospheric pressure is measured by a barometer or barograph. We monitor regularly the 
pressure value, especially his tendency. Level of atmospheric pressure changes with the 
weather situation ie. the atmospheric pressure in some way determines how the weather 
will be. So on arrival of air with low pressure usually means we will have bad weather (clouds 
development, rain), and in case of high pressure air - clear weather.

The sudden rise of the pressure (higher pressure) indicates that system is stabilizing (no 
precipitation), but with strong winds.  
The sudden drop of the pressure (lower pressure) indicates the arrival of the extremely bad 
weather and it is followed by precipitation and strong winds. 
 
The amount of water vapor in the air is called humidity. Humidity is measured by 
hygrometer percentage. 
 
Warm air can contain a greater amount of water vapor than cold air. 
During cold nights the air cannot retain as much moisture as the heated air and the steam 
is condensed to water drops - dew. If the temperature falls below 0° C water vapor freezes 
and turns into frost. As warm air rises, taking up more space (expandable), its heats up, 
spreads in a larger volume and is cooled. The steam is condensed into water droplets and 
clouds are formed. 
Another way of cooling air is in contact with something, for example, the ground. The 
consequence of this way of cooling is fog.  
 
7.2. AIR MASSES AND FRONTS 
 
Air mass is large mass of air that covers large area over earth’s surface and it is defined by 
its temperature and water vapour content. 
 
As regards to the characteristics of the substrate we distinguish hot and cold and wet or dry 
air mass. 
 
The pressure difference between the mass of air cause the airflow from the area of high 
pressure to low. This planetary movement of air masses cause the appearance of constant 
winds.  
When air mass moves we call it air current. 
 
Fronts are boundaries between air masses of different characteristics. 
 
There are three basic ways of rising of the air. 
 
The first is the slow rise of warm air over colder, what is happening next to the front. 
The second is rapid ascent of hot air in the convection currents. 
The third occurs when the air is forced to climb because of orographic barriers (hill, mountain 
range). 
 
Cold air is denser than average, while warm is less denser - column of cold air will be heavier 
than the same column of hot air. Weight spaced air through the unit area is called pressure 
but denser colder air will create higher pressure and, contrary rarer and warmer air will cause 
lower pressure.

Picture – cold, warm and occluded fronts 
 
Cold front is in front of the cold air mass that suppress horizontal warmer air mass (colder 
air is pushing warmer air). 
 
Warm front in front of a warmer air mass, which suppress horizontal colder air mass 
(warmer air is pushing colder air). 
 
Occluded front or occlusion is a complex front formed when a cold front overtakes the 
warm front, with the colder air mass underlines the warmer (two colder air masses connect 
at low levels and push warmer air upwards). Type of occluded fronts depends on the 
temperature of three different air masses, and of the way the front catching up. 
 
Stationary front is located between two air masses with different characteristics, but no 
tendency to move/push one another.

Picture - Synoptic chart of Europe 
 
7.3. CYCLONE 
 
Cyclones are areas of reduced (lower) atmospheric pressure relative to the surroundings. 
As air flows into the region of low pressure from all sides, the Coriolis force rotates the wind 
direction to the right.  
 
This produces a circulation of air counter clockwise in the northern hemisphere; the air is 
converging (inward) and is forced to pivot upwardly. 
 
The development of cyclones is divided into the initial phase, a young cyclone, mature 
cyclone and occluded phase. 
 
Precipitation brought by cyclones are arranged so that in the warm sector it is almost 
nonexistent, and are found mainly in the center and on the side of the cold front. 
 
Monitoring of cyclones is extremely important for each boat leader because of the possibility 
of creating a storm, the change in the strength and direction of the wind and precipitation.

Picture - cyclone from space on the northern and southern hemisphere 
 
7.4. ANTICYCLONE 
 
Anticyclone is an area of high atmospheric pressure in relation to the surroundings with 
almost circular isobars. In the areas of anti-cyclone the air is lowered to the surface and is 
forced to diverge (from the center). Coriolis force forces winds to rotate in a clockwise 
direction (Northern Hemisphere). 
 
Anticyclones usually cover large areas of air (3000 km), and does not contain fronts (unlike 
the cyclone). If there are clouds they evaporate. As the air descends closer to the ground, it 
is heated and at the ground becomes warm and dry. Anticyclone is moving slowly and can 
stay over some area for several weeks. In winter, it can lead to very low temperatures (temp. 
inversion), and in the summer can cause prolonged drought because anticyclone is blocking 
the arrival of cyclone or it diverts them. 
 
 
7.5. WINDS 
 
The winds are caused by the air particles moving from the area of high pressure to the low 
pressure area. 
Because of planetary temperature differences complex air movement and areas of high and 
low pressure are formed around the globe. Because of these belts of different pressures, 
we have trade winds. 
Cyclones and anticyclones also affect the winds.  
In the northern hemisphere due to the influence of the rotation of the Earth wind flows almost 
parallel to the isobars, in cyclones it is moving counter clockwise and in an anticyclone in 
the clockwise direction (in the southern hemisphere vice versa). When isobars on a chart 
closer or denser wind is stronger. 
 
Wind is determined by the direction and speed. Wind direction is estimated by using a 
pinwheel (compass rose), to the point of the horizon from where the wind is blowing. 
Speed is determined by the distance air particles travels per unit of time. Expressed in knots 
(nautical miles per hour - knt) or in meters per second (m/s), and measured with 
anemometer.

Most sailboats and yachts are equipped with a special device that constantly registers the 
direction and speed of wind. We have true, relative and apparent wind. 
True wind is the wind that blows regardless of our movement and direction of the bow.  
Relative wind is relative to direction of our bow. For example, if we are traveling in course 
of 90° and true wind is E (east), the direction of relative wind would be 0° (directly in to our 
bow).  
Apparent wind is wind that is generated by our movement in combination with the true 
wind. For example, if true wind is 0 (calm) and we are moving 5 knots in one direction, 
apparent wind would have same speed but 180° opposite direction.  
 
 
7.3. LOCAL WINDS 
 
The most famous local wind is sea breeze (cro. Zmorac). After the Sun goes up, it starts to 
warm up the land and the air above it. Above sea level the air is cooler because water 
absorbs solar radiation and heated slower then land. Warm air rises over land, but under it 
enters colder air from the sea. By mid-summer afternoon he can penetrate deep into the 
mainland. During the summer heat, the morning air is completely still, but already about 10-
12 hours sea breeze certainly appears. 
At night, the air tends to have the opposite movement. Just as fast as during the day land 
warms up, at night is cools. Often its temperature at night drops below the temperature of 
the sea, and the result is the emergence of the soft wind that blows from the mainland – 
land breeze. 
In coastal areas with mountains and hills, cold air is further pulled down by gravity so it 
generates strong downward winds – katabatic winds, like bura. 
 
Adriatic winds blow from the following 
directions: 
 
• N = north – tramuntana 
• NE = northeast – bura 
• E = east – levanat 
• SE = southeast – jugo 
• S = south – oštro 
• SW = southwest – lebić 
• W = west – punenat 
• N = northwest – mistral 
 
SW – NW = Nevera (neverin) 
 
 
Bura 
 
Bura is a very strong wind, which occasionally blows especially in the colder times of the 
year in the northern part of the eastern Adriatic coast, and in some other parts of the earth. 
 
Bura is coming from the land over the mountains towards the sea. Bura can have hurricane 
strength, especially there, where the mountains and hills are close to the seacoast.

Bura occurs in such way that cool air from flat land area behind mountain is moving over 
and slopes down the mountain range, towards the sea, and as the air flow, as it moves down 
the steep incline, it gains very high speed. It has a distinct vertical component of movement 
(hence the name downward wind – katabatic wind) and has severe restless flow of air or 
turbulence. This feature is reflected in particular in the fact that mainstream of the wind is 
not uniform, so that it blows in gusts, with the weaker and stronger blows gusts (cro. refuli) 
which change with a short or long break, during which wind weakens considerably. 
 
General preconditions for the bura are flat land, mountain range, and two climatically 
different, incoherent area (colder air from land and warmer air above the sea). 
 
It is important to stress out that bura creates water mist/water dust above the sea (water 
droplets so lightweight that are not affected by gravity). If you do not protect yourself, 
inhalation of spray can lead to "dry-drowning". 
 
Jugo 
 
Jugo is a warm and humid south-easterly wind blowing from south-south-east (S-SE) to 
east-south-east (E-SE). It is typically accompanied by rain as well as storms. It blows along 
the whole Adriatic coast, and is the strongest in the southern part causing the largest waves 
in the Adriatic Sea. During the summer time jugo blows with the speed of 4-5Bf (16-20kn) 
while in winter it can reach the speed up to 7-8Bf (27-40kn). Jugo is stronger on the open 
sea. Along the coast, however it becomes stronger primarily in the channels between the 
islands and on some sheltered sides of the islands, where it is, due to the formation of the 
surface, channelled. Jugo blows throughout the whole year, in the northern Adriatic more 
often from March to June, and in the southern Adriatic during the autumn and winter months. 
In the autumn and winter, it is a warm wind, in spring it is relatively cold, and in the summer 
it is steaming. Jugo usually lasts 2-3 days in the summer and up to 3 weeks in winter. It is 
characterized by long waves. 
 
We distinguish cyclonic jugo (accompanied by dense and low clouds with occasional, 
frequent and heavy rains) and anticyclonic jugo (blurry sky with dust from the deserts of 
North Africa and Asia, the air temperature is high, and poor air humidity). 
 
Maestral 
 
Mistral is a refreshing north-western wind in the warm day’s blows from the sea to the 
mainland. 
 
It occurs mainly in the summer and blows just along the coast and rarely reaches beyond 
20 M to the interior and it is a strictly surface wind (up to 300 m in height). Lasts about 10 
hours. Around 14:00 maestral reaches its maximum and always ends before sunset (usually 
around 18 hours). Maestral is accompanied by good weather and at the same time 
significantly alleviates the summer sultriness. Normally blows as weak wind (up to 4 Bf).

7.4. STORM 
 
Storm is extremely bad weather with a strong wind. According to the Beaufort scale, the 
stormy wind is the strength of the 8 Bf, which cuts smaller branches from the trees and 
prevents the walk. At sea, the wind is accompanied by moderately high waves, in which the 
edges of the crest are broken and vortices, and the foam is decomposed in well-defined 
strands along the direction of the wind. The wind reaches a speed of 17 to 21 m/s (60 to 75 
km/h). There is a thunderstorm, often accompanied by rainfalls, a fierce storm , for which, in 
the event of a stormy wind, a snowstorm, for which, in the event of a stormy wind snow is 
falling, dust or sandstorms, for which the wind storms carries large amounts of dust or sand. 
 
Storm is a disturbance in the atmosphere, which causes significant changes in the wind, 
pressure and temperature spatial proportions that range from the size of a tornado (diameter 
of 1 kilometer ) to the extantropic cyclones (diameter from 3,000 to 5,000 kilometers). Winds 
faster than 32.7 m/s or 12 Bf are called hurricanes. 
 
Nevera signifies the bad weather, or the weather that is very bad for staying at sea. Nevera 
can mean a sudden storm at sea (a storm with lightning and thunderstorms or a storm with 
thunder and rain), a short-term storm caused by great heat. The notion of nevera signifies 
the hurricane power of the wind that can sink the ships in its encounter, pull the trees, break 
the roofs of the houses. 
 
For vessels sudden storms (lesser and shorter ones) are dangerous and that can 
occasionally create unpleasantly high waves.

7.8. CLOUDS 
 
A cloud is an aerosol consisting of a visible mass of small liquid droplets, frozen crystals, 
or other particles suspended in the atmosphere of a planetary body. The air which rises up 
in the upper atmosphere is diluted and cools, condensation occurs and the formation of 
clouds appear. Type of cloud depends on the level of condensation and on the cause of the 
vertical currents. 
 
High clouds form at altitudes above 6000m 
• C - cirrus clouds are composed of ice crystals and they are the sign of the changes of the 
weather, and the path of their movement corresponds to the direction of incoming 
cyclones. 
• Cc - cirrocumulus clouds (sheep) quickly change the look and predict the strengthening 
of the wind in the lower atmosphere 
• Cs - cirrostratus clouds regularly are foretelling bad weather. 
 
Middle clouds at an altitude of 2000-6000m 
• Ac - altocumulus clouds do not give precipitation, they predict a strengthening of the wind 
in the lower atmosphere. 
• As - altostratus clouds are similar to the Cs clouds in summer, provides little rain in winter 
or snow. 
 
 
Picture - Types of clouds 
 
Low clouds on height under 2000m 
• Ns - nimbostratus cloud, thick dark layer to dark appearance of the sky from which 
regularly we get rain/snow. 
• Sc - stratocumulus cloud, thick and dark wavy cloud mass, but not giving precipitation. 
• St - stratus is uniform, grey, low cloud, which is usually located at a height between 0.2 
and 0.5 kilometers. Gives substantially no precipitation, and if given, are mostly in the

form of fine particles such as drizzle, snow or ice crystal grains. If stratus is in the ground, 
it is called fog. 
 
The clouds of vertical development 
• Cu - cumulus cloud, white and dense tufts, hover over the mainland and islands, the sign 
of good weather. 
• Cb - cumulonimbus cloud is a cloud of strong vertical development, so-called, 
thundercloud, large cloud mass peaks with a cauliflower-like shape and a cap of the anvil. 
Bring precipitation in just one area, hail with lightning and thunder.  
 
 
7.9. FOG AND VISIBILITY 
 
Visibility is the greatest horizontal distance (in km) where you can see and distinguish 
surrounding objects (light). Fog (cro. magla) is floating tiny water drops in the lower layer of 
air, which reduce horizontal visibility to less than 1km. 
When visibility is between 1 and 10 kilometers, it is called mist (cro. sumaglica). First, mist 
occurs, but only by increasing the relative humidity of air we have fog.t. 
Dense fog reduces visibility up to 50m. Moderate 50 - 500m and the weak from 500 - 1000m. 
 
 
7.10. TIDE 
 
Due to the gravitational attraction of the Sun and Moon water on Earth's surface is collected 
in two well-defined "waves". Gravity acts in a straight line, however, one of these waves is 
on the side closer to the Moon, and the other on the opposite side. On the other axis 
therefore arise depressions. These waves are called tidal waves. 
 
 
 
Picture - The influence of the Sun and Moon on the water on Earth 
 
Tidal waves are not stationary because the Earth rotates on its axis, so the Moon comes 
over every meridian every 24h and 50min (lunar day). The result are two tides in every place 
in that period. How high water falls short of the culmination of the Moon, the actual tide is 
going to happen 6h after the passage of the Moon through the meridian and low tide is about 
the time of culmination.

In addition to the Moon and the Sun attracts the Earth water, but with a smaller force. Thus, 
the relative position of the Earth, Sun and Moon impact the tides. Every month two times it 
happens that the Sun, Moon and Earth are in a straight line (when the Moon is full or new) 
but the attractive force of the Sun adds or subtracts the lunar and the resulting tidal are 
greater or lower than the average. 
 
When the Sun and Moon are working together on one line we have spring tides. Between 
that, the Sun and Moon are at right angles so attraction of the Sun reduces the attraction of 
the Moon. The high tide is lower, and the low tide is greater than average. These are called 
neap tides. 
 
Before using nautical charts it is important to review which sea level between the tides are 
used to displayed depth on the chart (not the same on the charts published by HHI and 
elsewhere in the world because the Adriatic Sea is closed and within the Adriatic sea level 
of  tides have some exceptional fluctuations). 
 
7.11. WEATHER FORECAST 
 
Monitoring and interpretation of weather situations is an important part of the sailor's skills. 
It is just as important as knowledge of the operation or the correct positioning of the boat.  
 
Depending on the area of navigation and its duration before the departure of the sea always 
check the weather situation for the required area and the estimated duration of the trip. 
 
Keep track of barometric pressure and its tendency, temperature, type of clouds, sea state 
and wave, and actually prevailing conditions in the local area. 
 
The maritime-safety information (Maritime Safety Information - MSI) are broadcast by all 
three coastal radio stations (Rijeka, Split, Dubrovnik) via VHF system in English and 
Croatian language, and ORP Split Radio via Navtex system in English. NAVTEX messages 
of international importance are submitted to the NAVAREA coordinator in English. 
 
NAVTEX (Navigational Telex) system is part of the Global Maritime Distress and Safety 
System (GMDSS - Global Maritime Distress and Safety System), used for broadcasting 
maritime safety information (MSI) in the framework of the World Service of maritime safety 
information (WWNWS - World Wide Navigational Warning Service). Operating frequency on 
which the broadcast message is done is 518 kHz in FEC mode or mode with improved error 
correction. In some countries is used and 490 kHz frequency in the local language.

8. MANEUVERING 
 
8.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
Maneuvering is term used for actions comprising of using propulsion means (engine, sails, 
oars) and rudder, individually or combined in order to bring the boat to the desired position 
or to maintain the desired direction. During manoeuvring we must also take account of the 
navigational characteristics of the boat, as well as hydro-meteorological conditions. 
Improperly loaded boat has less of navigation and manoeuvring characteristics, the 
manoeuvring is more difficult. 
 
First sailing with boat should be done in calm seas and to test or examine behavior 
(maneuverability) of the boat at various speeds during navigation:  
 
• Size of turning circle 
• Duration of a half-turn and turn around, 
• Length of time and distance to full stop; with just stopping the engine and using astern 
propulsion, 
• Time to switch from driving forward to driving astern, 
• The ability to move astern due to the transverse shift of stern, 
• How the boat behaves when it is stopped; due to wind, ocean currents and waves, 
• How the wind affects the boat while moving astern, 
• Turning ability in a small space. 
 
A rudder is a primary control surface used to steer a ship or boat that moves through a fluid 
medium. A rudder operates by redirecting the fluid past the hull (watercraft) or fuselage, thus 
imparting a turning or yawing motion to the craft. In basic form, a rudder is a flat plane or 
sheet of material attached with hinges to the craft's stern, tail, or after end. Often rudders 
are shaped to minimize hydrodynamic or aerodynamic drag.  
 
There are three types of steering rudders: 
 
• balanced - most of the rudder is before the shaft 10% (More than 10% of rudder surface 
is in front of the shaft (axis)) 
• semi balanced - small part prior to the shaft up to 10% (Less than 10% of rudder surface 
is in front of the shaft (axis)) 
• unbalanced - entire surface of the rudder behind the shaft 
 
 
 
Picture – Balanced, semibalanced and unbalanced

Rudder has its maximum efficiency at approximately 45 degrees offset, however, the largest 
real efficiency is between 30 and 35 degrees in moving forward and while driving astern 
rudder biggest effect is at an angle of about 20 degrees. 
 
A propeller is the most common propulsor on ships, imparting momentum to a fluid which 
causes a force to act on the ship. 
 
We distinguish between right-handed and left-handed propeller with respect to rotation to a 
driving forward, as viewed from the stern to the bow. In driving forward, the rear side of the 
propeller is called pressure side, and front surface - suction side. The tip of propeller is the 
farthest point from the axis of the propeller. We distinguish between the leading edge and 
trailing edge of each blade. 
 
 
 
We have vessels with one, two or more propellers. The propellers may have two, three or 
more blades. 
 
When moving astern with right-turning propeller transverse shift of stern is more pronounced 
to the left side. The impact of the transverse shift due to propeller is greater on vessels with 
smaller draft, and more pronounced if part of propeller rises above the sea.
