8.2. MANEUVERING 
 
In moving forward the boat is regularly well managed but while driving astern we should 
achieve a certain speed so steering has an effect. 
 
Shift of stern caused by propeller can increase or reduce the turning circle depending on 
whether we turn the rudder to the side of transverse shift of stern or vice versa. 
 
 
Turning circle of the ship in the direction opposite of rotation of the propeller is much smaller 
- ship with right turning propeller moving forward makes a smaller turning circle to the left. 
This phenomenon is even more pronounced when moving astern, so it is very difficult for 
some ships to turn to the side of the rotation of the propeller so the only solution is rapidly 
add and subtract engine power forward and back, during which time the rudder is kept 
constantly in the same position. 
 
Turning circle of the ship is circle that ship makes at constant speed with the same rudder 
angle. Besides being dependent on the rudder angle and speed, depends on the draft and 
the length of the ship. The point around which the ship turns around is called a waypoint. 
 
• Rudder is turned to the right  
• Stern of the ship is shifted to the left because 
of the strong force acting on the stern 
• Bow of the ship due to inertia is trying to 
keep the original movement, therefore the 
transverse shift of stern is more pronounced 
• 1-2: ship drift sideways to the opposite side 
of the rudder (2 to 3 length L) 
• 2 - 3: bow turns to the desired direction (3 -4 
L) 
• 3 - 4: the route is roughly equal to the circle 
 
 
When turning the ships bow is facing inside and stern is being shifted outside. On the outside 
side of the stern we have high pressure and low on the inside, resulting in the strong currents 
that reduces speed (by 20 to 40%). In addition the ship will be initially inclined to the side of 
the rudder, while during second part of the turn it will lean on the side opposite from the 
rudder.

8.3. NAVIGATION 
 
Boat should be prepared, check the state of fuel and supplies (water), equipment, 
documents and certificates, navigation aids, know the weather forecast. Whether your ship 
is bigger or smaller, sailing boat or motor boat one should approach it systematically and 
consistently avoiding superficiality and rashness. It is best to make, especially for 
inexperienced sailors, a special checklist with a list of tasks and checks prior to departure. 
Cruise vacation searches for even better organization and special preparations and check 
your routes and choose the safest route (shoals, reefs) taking into account the inventory, 
the number of passengers, arrival time at a particular port, local peculiarities and other 
details so that cruise passes safely in complete satisfaction. 
 
Passenger vessel cruise trip should be even better organized. Especially planning the safest 
route (shoals, reefs) taking into account supplies, number of passengers, ETA, local 
highlights which can provide a safe and satisfactory cruise. 
 
8.4. DEPARTURE 
 
         
 
 
Picture – Departure manoeuvres 
 
Warm up the engine; determine the direction and strength of the wind in the port and as well 
condition of the sea outside the port. Reinforce all moving parts. Close all openings that are 
not necessary. Check the lights. Observe the situation in the marinas and think about the 
best way to departure. Prepare backup manoeuvre. 
 
Untie and fold all the ropes except two, bow and aft line, or spring line depending on the 
situation. 
 
Depending on the wind direction and speed, push away boat from the shore using hook or 
by slightly using engine on the spring; pull over to the anchor at the bow or stern. When the 
boat is moved away a little use boat engine and rudder to gently pull the boat away from the 
mooring spot. Beware of other boats as well as on your own.

Boats that are leaving marina always have the right of passage before boats entering the 
harbour. Once we leave marina use economic speed according to the plan of sailing. 
Always follow the Rules for Preventing Collisions at Sea and port regulations. 
 
8.5. ENTERING THE PORT 
 
In the selection of mooring point should be taken into account as an easier departure (if 
possible). In major ports, there is a prescribed order and the dock can be only along part of 
the coast that is reserved for boats. Therefore, prior to arrival check Sailing directions of that 
area (Pilot books) and special regulations relating to the respective port, and if necessary 
contact (by VHF) harbourmaster's office or marina. 
Before entering boat needs to be prepared for mooring: lower the fenders on the side of the 
docking prepare the ropes and boat hook. It is good to lower the anchor to a certain level 
and must remain ready for lowering if something goes wrong due to wind or too fast arrival. 
If you can choose, select a mooring spot on the side of the shift of the propeller or one that 
enables the easiest spot to get underway. 
 
8.6. ANCHORING 
 
Before arriving at the anchorage you should study the navigation chart (plan) and Pilot book, 
and on that basis, and taking into account the weather, choose a location and anchor 
lowering point. 
The anchor point is determined by good orientation - covered directions, measuring and 
evaluation of distance. 
 
When you arrive at the anchorage you should pay attention to ships that are already 
anchored as your ship after anchoring will be set in the same direction as those ships. We 
have to take into account that the ships rotate around its anchor so that we leave enough 
space around us in case of change of wind. Of course, in that case all boats will be rotated 
in the same direction. The radius of the circle that describes our ship is the length of the 
chain. For safe anchoring chain length is considered to be 4 to 6 the amount of depth of the 
sea. 
 
The anchor holds best on the muddy-sandy bottom. If you do not know what the sea bottom 
is or you are anchoring at night, be sure to fasten small additional rope to anchor. Anchor at 
a sufficient distance from the mainland, shoals and other boats. During the night the wind 
direction can change and also low tide can come. 
Anchoring is done with moving slightly ahead from the anchor release point, stop the boat, 
drop anchor to the bottom, and then move astern until anchor rope is extended. 
 
8.7. BOAT LEADER AND CREW 
 
The commander of the boat / yacht must always bear in mind that before any maneuver 
he/she must explain to crew members what they do. You must be convinced that they have 
these instructions and requirements properly understood. Upon execution of the maneuver 
start when everybody is ready, and equipment is in place. 
After the maneuver, check that all is done in the best way, and if necessary, correct. 
Explain the crew what was good and what was poorly executed and soon you will have great 
co-workers.

9. RULES ON PREVENTING COLLISIONS AT SEA 
 
9.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea are a set of international legal norms regulating 
maritime transport. Adopted on the Conference by the IMO, and in effect from 15.07.1977. 
This policy emphasizes the obligation of the navigator to behave in accordance with 
maritime practice and circumstances of the case, and that within the application of the rules 
no one can be relieved of the consequences in case of any omission. Seafarers are required 
to fully know the Regulations for Preventing Collisions. There is no time to check the book 
when risk is imminent. 
 
Definitions: 
 
a) The word "vessel" includes every description of watercraft, including non-displacement 
craft, WIG craft and seaplanes, used or capable of being used as a means of transportation 
on water. 
 
(b) The term "power-driven vessel" means any vessel propelled by machinery. 
 
(c) The term "sailing vessel" means any vessel under sail provided that propelling 
machinery, if fitted, is not being used. 
 
(d) The term "vessel engaged in fishing" means any vessel fishing with nets, lines, trawls 
or other fishing apparatus which restricts manoeuvrability, but does not include a vessel 
fishing with trolling lines or other fishing apparatus which do not restrict manoeuvrability. 
 
(e) The term "seaplane" includes any aircraft designed to manoeuvre on the water. 
 
(f) The term "vessel not under command" means a vessel which through some 
exceptional circumstance is unable to manoeuvre as required by these Rules and is 
therefore unable to keep out of the way of another vessel. 
 
(g) The term "vessel restricted in her ability to manoeuvre" means a vessel which from 
the nature of her work is restricted in her ability to manoeuvre as required by these Rules 
and is therefore unable to keep out of the way of another vessel. The term "vessels restricted 
in their ability to manoeuvre" shall include but not be limited to: 
 
(i) a vessel engaged in laying, servicing or picking up a navigation mark, submarine cable 
or pipeline; 
(ii) a vessel engaged in dredging, surveying or underwater operations; 
(iii) a vessel engaged in replenishment or transferring persons, provisions or cargo while 
underway; 
(iv) a vessel engaged in launching or recovery of aircraft; 
(v) a vessel engaged in mine clearance operations; 
(vi) a vessel engaged in a towing operation such as severely restricts the towing vessel and 
her tow in their ability to deviate from their course.

(h) The term "vessel constrained by her draught" means a power-driven vessel which, 
because of her draught in relation to available depth and width of navigable water, is 
severely restricted in her ability to deviate from the course she is following. 
 
(i) The word "underway" means that a vessel is not at anchor, or made fast to the shore, 
or aground. 
 
(j) The words "length" and "breadth" of a vessel mean her length overall and greatest 
breadth. 
 
(k) Vessel shall be deemed to be in sight of one another only when one can be observed 
visually from the other. 
 
(l) The term "restricted visibility" means any condition in which visibility is restricted by 
fog, mist, falling snow, heavy rainstorms, sandstorms or any other similar causes. 
 
(m) The term "Wing-In-Ground (WIG) craft" means a multimodal craft which, in its main 
operational mode, flies in close proximity to the surface by utilizing surface-effect action. 
 
9.2. RESPONSIBILITY 
 
Nothing in COLREGs rules shall exonerate any vessel, or the owner, master or crew thereof, 
from the consequences of any neglect to comply with these Rules or of the neglect of any 
precautions which may be required by the ordinary practice of seamen, or by the special 
circumstances of the case. 
 
In construing and complying with these Rules due regard shall be had to all dangers of 
navigation and collision and to any special circumstances, including the limitations of the 
vessels involved, which may make a departure from these Rules necessary to avoid 
immediate danger. 
 
9.3. LOOK-OUT 
 
Every vessel shall at all times maintain a proper look-out by sight and hearing as well as by 
all available means appropriate in the prevailing circumstances and conditions so as to make 
a full appraisal of the situation and or the risk of collision. 
 
9.4. SAFETY SPEED 
 
Every vessel shall at all times proceed at a safe speed so that she can take proper and 
effective action to avoid a collision and be stopped within a distance appropriate to the 
prevailing circumstances and conditions. 
 
9.5. RISK OF COLLISION 
 
Every vessel shall use all available means appropriate to the prevailing circumstances and 
conditions to determine if risk of collision exists. If there is any doubt such risk shall be 
deemed to exist. Proper use shall be made of radar equipment if fitted and operational, 
including long-range scanning to obtain early warning of risk of collision and radar plotting

or equivalent systematic observations of detected objects. Assumptions shall not be made 
on the basis of scanty information, especially scanty radar information.  
 
In determining if risk of collision exists the following considerations shall be among those 
taken into account: 
 
(i) such risk shall be deemed to exist it the compass bearing of an approaching vessel does 
not appreciably change; 
(ii) such risk may sometimes exist even when an appreciable bearing change is evident, 
particularly when approaching a very large vessel or a tow or when approaching a vessel at 
close range. 
 
Any action taken to avoid collision shall be taken in accordance with the Rules of this Part 
and shall, if the circumstances of the case admit, be positive, made in ample time and with 
due regard to the observance of good seamanship. 
 
Any alteration of course and/or speed to avoid collision shall, if the circumstances of the 
case admit, be large enough to be readily apparent to another vessel observing visually or 
by radar; a succession of small alterations of course and/or speed should be avoided. 
 
9.6. SAILING SHIPS 
 
When two sailing vessels are approaching one another, to involve risk of collision, one of 
them shall keep out of the way of the other as follows: 
 
(i) when each has the wind on a different side, the vessel which has the wind on the port 
side shall keep out of the way of the other; 
(ii) when both have the wind on the same side, the vessel which is to windward shall keep 
out of the way of the vessel which is to leeward; 
(iii) if a vessel with the wind on the port side sees a vessel to windward and cannot determine 
with certainty whether the other vessel has the wind on her port or starboard side, she shall 
keep out of the way of the other. 
 
For the purposes of this Rule the windward side shall be deemed to be the side opposite to 
that on which the mainsail is carried or, in the case of a square-rigged vessel, the side 
opposite to that on which the largest fore-and-aft sail is carried.

9.7. OVERTAKING 
 
Any vessel overtaking any other shall keep out of the way of the vessel being overtaken. 
 
A vessel shall be deemed to be overtaking when coming up with another vessel from a 
direction more than 22.5° abaft her beam, that is, in such a position with reference to the 
vessel she is overtaking, that at night she would be able to see only the stern light of that 
vessel but neither of her sidelights. 
 
When a vessel is in any doubt as to whether she is overtaking another, she shall assume 
that this is the case and act accordingly. 
 
Any subsequent alteration of the bearing between the two vessels shall not make the 
overtaking vessel a crossing vessel within the meaning of these Rules or relieve her of the 
duty of keeping clear of the overtaken vessel until she is finally past and clear. 
 
9.8. HEAD-ON SITUATION 
 
When two power-driven vessels are meeting on reciprocal or nearly reciprocal courses so 
as to involve risk of collision each shall alter her course to starboard so that each shall pass 
on the port side of the other. 
 
Such a situation shall be deemed to exist when a vessel sees the other ahead or nearly 
ahead and by night she could see the masthead lights of the other in line or nearly in a line 
and/or both sidelights and by day she observes the corresponding aspect of the other 
vessel. 
 
When a vessel is in any doubt as to whether such a situation exists she shall assume that it 
does exist and act accordingly. 
 
9.9. CROSSING SITUATION 
 
When two power-driven vessels are crossing so as to involve risk of collision, the vessel 
which has the other on her own starboard side shall keep out of the way and shall, if the 
circumstances of the case admit, avoid crossing ahead of the other vessel. 
 
9.10. ACTION BY GIVE-WAY VESSEL 
 
Every vessel which is directed to keep out of the way of another vessel shall, as far as 
possible, take early and substantial action to keep well clear. 
 
9.11. ACTION BY STAND-ON VESSEL 
 
Where one of two vessels is to keep out of the way the other shall keep her course and 
speed. The latter vessel may, however, take action to avoid collision by her manoeuvre 
alone, as soon as it becomes apparent to her that the vessel required to keep out of the way 
is not taking appropriate action in compliance with these Rules.

When, from any cause, the vessel required to keep her course and speed finds herself so 
close that collision cannot be avoided by the action of the give-way vessel alone, she shall 
take such action as will best aid to avoid collision. 
 
A power-driven vessel which takes action in a crossing situation in accordance with this Rule 
to avoid collision with another power-driven vessel shall, if the circumstances at the case 
admit, not alter course to port for a vessel on her own port side. 
 
This Rule does not relieve the give-way vessel of her obligation to keep out of the way. Every 
ship, other than a ship cannot maneuver the ship or limited maneuverability, must not 
interfere with the safe passage of the ship limited its draft. 
 
9.12. CONDUCT OF VESSELS IN RESTRICTED VISIBILITY 
 
This Rule applies to vessels not in sight of one another when navigating in or near an area 
of restricted visibility. 
 
Every vessel shall proceed at a safe speed adapted to the prevailing circumstances and 
conditions of restricted visibility. A power-driven vessel shall have her engines ready for 
immediate manoeuvre. 
 
Every vessel shall have due regard to the prevailing circumstances and conditions of 
restricted visibility when complying with the Rules of Section I of this Part. 
 
A vessel which detects by radar alone the presence of another vessel shall determine if a 
close-quarters situation is developing and/or risk of collision exists. 
 
9.13. LIGHTS 
 
 
 
Picture - boat navigation lights

→ Mast head lights - continuous white light, Sector 225°, set in the fore and aft. 
→ Sidelights - steady green on the right, a permanent red on the left side, the sector of 
112.5°. 
→ Stern light - continuous white light, Sector 135°, raised stern. 
→ Towing light - yellow light of the same characteristics as the stern light 
→ A circular light - constant light, sector 360°. 
→ Flashing light - 120 flashes per minute 
 
(a) "Masthead light" means a white light placed over the fore-and-aft centreline of the vessel 
showing an unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 225° and so fixed as to show the 
light from right ahead to 22.5° abaft the beam on either side of the vessel. 
(b) "Sidelights" means a green light on the starboard side and a red light on the port side 
each showing an unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 112.5° and so fixed as to show 
the light from right ahead to 22.5° abaft the beam on its respective side. In a vessel of less 
than 20 m in length the sidelights may be combined in one lantern carried on the fore-and-
aft centreline of the vessel. 
 
(c) "Stern light" means a white light placed as nearly as practicable at the stern showing an 
unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 135° and so fixed as to show the light 67.5° from 
right aft on each side of the vessel. 
 
(d) "Towing light" means a yellow light having the same characteristics as the "stern light" 
defined in paragraph (c) of this Rule. 
 
(e) "All-round light" means a light showing an unbroken light over an arc of the horizon of 
360°. 
 
(f) "Flashing light" means a light flashing at regular intervals at a frequency of 120 flashes or 
more per minute. 
 
9.14. POWER-DRIVEN VESSELS UNDERWAY 
 
A power-driven vessel underway shall exhibit: 
 
(i) a masthead light forward; 
(ii) a second masthead light abaft of and higher than the forward one; except that a vessel 
of less than 50 m in length shall not be obliged to exhibit such light but may do so; 
(iii) sidelights; 
(iv) a stern light.  
 
(b) An air-cushion vessel when operating in the non-displacement mode shall, in addition to 
the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of this Rule, exhibit an all-round flashing yellow light. 
(c) A WIG craft only when taking off, landing and in flight near the surface shall, in addition 
to the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of this Rule, exhibit a high intensity all-round flashing 
red light.  
(d) 
 
(i) A power-driven vessel of less than 12 m in length may in lieu of the lights prescribed in 
paragraph (a) of this Rule exhibit an all-round white light and sidelights;

(ii) a power-driven vessel of less than 7 m in length whose maximum speed does not exceed 
7 knots may in lieu of the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of this Rule exhibit an all-round 
white light and shall, if practicable, also exhibit sidelights;  
(iii) the masthead light or all-round white light on a power-driven vessel of less than 12 m in 
length may be displaced from the fore-and-aft centreline of the vessel if centreline fitting is 
not practicable, provided that the sidelights are combined in one lantern which shall be 
carried on the fore-and-aft centreline of the vessel or located as nearly as practicable in the 
same fore-and-aft line as the masthead light or the all-round white light. 
 
9.15. TOWING AND PUSHING 
 
A power-driven vessel when towing shall exhibit instead of the light prescribed in 9.14., two 
masthead lights in a vertical line. When the length of the tow, measured from the stern of 
the towing vessel to the after end of the tow exceeds 200 m, three such lights in a vertical 
line sidelights; a stern light; a towing light in a vertical line above the stern light; at daytime 
when the length of the tow exceeds 200 m, a diamond shape where it can best be seen. 
 
 
 
9.16. SAILING VESSELS UNDERWAY AND VESSELS UNDER OARS 
 
(a) A sailing vessel underway shall exhibit sidelights and a stern light. 
 
(b) In a sailing vessel of less than 20 m in length, the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of 
this Rule may be combined in one lantern at or near the top of the mast where it can best 
be seen. 
 
A sailing vessel underway may, in addition to the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of this 
Rule, exhibit at or near the top of the mast, where they can best be seen, two all-round lights 
in a vertical line, the upper being red and the lower green, but these lights shall not be 
exhibited in conjunction with the combined lantern permitted by paragraph (b) of this Rule. 
 
A sailing vessel of less than 7 m in length shall, if practicable, exhibit the lights prescribed in 
paragraph (a) or (b) of this Rule, but if she does not, she shall have ready at hand an electric 
torch or lighted lantern showing a white light that shall be exhibited in sufficient time to 
prevent collision. 
 
A vessel under oars may exhibit the lights prescribed in this Rule for sailing vessels, but if 
she does not, she shall have ready at hand an electric torch or lighted lantern showing a 
white light that shall be exhibited in sufficient time to prevent a collision. 
 
A vessel proceeding under sail when also being propelled by machinery shall exhibit forward 
where it can best be seen a conical shape, apex downwards.

9.17. FISHING VESSELS 
 
a) A vessel engaged in fishing, whether underway or at anchor, shall exhibit only the lights 
and shapes prescribed in this Rule. 
 
(b) A vessel when engaged in trawling, by which is meant the dragging through the water of 
a dredge net or other apparatus used as a fishing appliance, shall exhibit: 
 
(i) two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being green and the lower white, or a shape 
consisting of two cones with their apexes together in a vertical line one above the other; 
(ii) a masthead light abaft of and higher that the all-round green light; a vessel of less than 
50 metres in length shall not be obliged to exhibit such a light but may do so; 
 
(iii) when making way through the water, in addition to the lights prescribed in this paragraph, 
sidelights and a stern light. 
 
(c) A vessel engaged in fishing, other than trawling shall exhibit: 
 
(i) two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being red and the lower white, or a shape 
consisting of two cones with their apexes together in a vertical line one above the other; 
(ii) when there is outlying gear extending more than 150 m horizontally from the vessel, an 
all-round white light or a cone apex upwards in the direction of the gear; 
(iii) when making way through the water, in addition to the lights prescribed in this paragraph, 
sidelights and a stern light. 
 
(d) The additional signals described in Annex II to these Regulations apply to a vessel 
engaged in fishing in close proximity to other vessels engaged in fishing. 
 
(e) A vessel when not engaged in fishing shall not exhibit the lights or shapes prescribed in 
this Rule, but only those prescribed for a vessel of her length. 
 
9.18. VESSELS NOT UNDER COMMAND OR RESTRICTED IN THEIR 
ABILITY TO MANOEUVRE 
 
(a) A vessel not under command shall exhibit: 
 
(i) two all-round red lights in a vertical line where they can best be seen; 
(ii) two balls or similar shapes in a vertical line where they can best be seen; 
(iii) when making way through the water, in addition to the lights prescribed in this paragraph, 
sidelights and a stern light. 
 
(b) A vessel restricted in her ability to manoeuvre, except a vessel engaged in mine 
clearance operations, shall exhibit: 
 
(i) three all-round lights in a vertical line where they can best be seen. The highest and 
lowest of these lights shall be red and the middle light shall be white; 
(ii) three shapes in a vertical line where they can best be seen. The highest and lowest of 
these shapes shall be balls and the middle one a diamond;

(iii) when making way through the water, a masthead light or lights, sidelights and a stern 
light, in addition to the lights prescribed in subparagraph (i); 
(iv) when at anchor, in addition to the lights or shapes prescribed in subparagraphs (i) and 
(ii), the light, lights or shape prescribed in 9.20.. 
 
(c) A power-driven vessel engaged in a towing operation such as severely restricts the 
towing vessel and her tow in their ability to deviate from their course shall, in addition to the 
lights and shapes prescribed in 9.14. (a), exhibit the lights or shapes prescribed in 
subparagraphs (b)(i) and (ii) of this Rule. 
 
(d) A vessel engaged in dredging or underwater operations, when restricted in her ability to 
manoeuvre, shall exhibit the lights and shapes prescribed in subparagraph(b)(i), (ii) and (iii) 
of this Rule and shall in addition, when an obstruction exists, exhibit: 
 
(i) two all-round red lights or two balls in a vertical line to indicate the side on which the 
obstruction exists; 
(ii) two all-round green lights or two diamonds in a vertical line to indicate the side on which 
another vessel may pass; 
(iii) when at anchor, the lights or shapes prescribed in this paragraph instead of the lights or 
shape prescribed in 9.20.. 
 
(e) Whenever the size of a vessel engaged in diving operations makes it impracticable to 
exhibit all the lights and shapes prescribed in the paragraph (d) of this Rule, the following 
shall be exhibited: 
 
(i) three all-round lights in a vertical line where they can best be seen. The highest and 
lowest of these lights shall be red and the middle light shall be white; 
(ii) a rigid replica of the International Code flag "A" not less than 1 metre in height. Measures 
shall be taken to ensure its all-round visibility. 
 
Smaller ships with divers underwater must highlight flag that is not in the maritime alphabet. 
 
 
 
Picture - flags that warn of divers under water 
 
(f) A vessel engaged in mine clearance operations shall in addition to the lights prescribed 
for a power-driven vessel in Rule 9.13. or to the lights or shape prescribed for a vessel at

anchor in 9.20. as appropriate, exhibit three all-round green lights or three balls. One of 
these lights or shapes shall be exhibited near the foremast head and one at each end of the 
fore yard. These lights or shapes indicate that it is dangerous for another vessel to approach 
within 1000 m of the mine clearance vessel. 
 
(g) Vessels of less than 12 m in length, except those engaged in diving operations, shall not 
be required to exhibit the lights and shapes prescribed in this Rule. 
 
9.19. VESSELS CONSTRAINED BY THEIR DRAUGHT 
 
A vessel constrained by her draught may, in addition to the lights prescribed for power-
driven vessels in 9.13., exhibit where they can best be seen three all-round red lights in a 
vertical line, or a cylinder. 
 
9.20. PILOT VESSELS 
 
a) A vessel engaged on pilotage duty shall exhibit: 
 
(i) at or near the masthead, two all-round lights in a vertical line, the upper being white and 
the lower red; 
 
(ii) when underway, in addition, sidelights and a stern light; 
 
(iii) when at anchor, in addition to the lights prescribed in sub-paragraph (i), the light, lights 
or shape prescribed in 9.20. for vessels at anchor. 
 
(b) A pilot vessel when not engaged on pilotage duty shall exhibit the lights or shapes 
prescribed for a similar vessel of her length. 
 
There are no shapes prescribed for vessel engaged on pilotage duty, however it is common 
that pilot vessel exhibit International Code flag “H” and/or have letter “P” or word “PILOT” 
painted on its hull or superstructure. 
 
9.21. ANCHORED VESSELS AND VESSELS AGROUND 
 
(a) A vessel at anchor shall exhibit where it can best be seen: 
 
(i) in the fore part, an all-round white light or one ball; 
 
(ii) at or near the stern and at a lower level that the light prescribed in subparagraph (i), an 
all-round white light. 
 
(b) A vessel of less than 50 m in length may exhibit an all-round white light where it can best 
be seen instead of the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) of this Rule. 
 
(c) A vessel at anchor may, and a vessel of 100 m and more in length shall, also use the 
available working or equivalent lights to illuminate her decks.

(d) A vessel aground shall exhibit the lights prescribed in paragraph (a) or (b) of this Rule 
and in addition, where they can best be seen: 
 
(i) two all-round red lights in a vertical line; 
 
(ii) three balls in a vertical line. 
 
(e) A vessel of less than 7 m in length, when at anchor, not in or near a narrow channel, 
fairway or anchorage, or where other vessels normally navigate, shall not be required to 
exhibit the lights or shape prescribed in paragraphs (a) and (b) of this Rule. 
 
(f) A vessel of less than 12 m in length, when aground, shall not be required to exhibit the 
lights or shapes prescribed in subparagraphs (d)(i) and (ii) of this Rule. 
 
9.22. SOUND AND LIGHT SIGNALS 
 
The word "whistle" means any sound signalling appliance capable of producing the 
prescribed blasts 
 
The term "short blast" means a blast of about one second duration. 
 
The term "prolonged blast" means a blast of from four to six seconds duration. 
 
A vessel of 12 m or more in length shall be provided with a whistle, a vessel of 20 m or more 
in length shall be provided with a bell in addition to a whistle, and a vessel of 100 m or more 
in length shall, in addition, be provided with a gong, the tone and sound of which cannot be 
confused with that of the bell. The whistle, bell and gong shall comply with the specifications 
in Annex III to these Regulations. The bell or gong or both may be replaced by other 
equipment having the same respective sound characteristics, provided that manual 
sounding of the prescribed signals shall always be possible. 
 
A vessel of less than 12 m in length shall not be obliged to carry the sound signalling 
appliances prescribed in paragraph (a) of this Rule but if she does not, she shall be provided 
with some other means of making an efficient sound signal. 
 
When vessel are in sight of one another, a power-driven vessel underway, when 
manoeuvring as authorised or required by these Rules, shall indicate that manoeuvre by the 
following signals on her whistle: 
 
- one short blast to mean "I am altering my course to starboard"; 
 
- two short blasts to mean "I am altering my course to port"; 
 
- three short blasts to mean "I am operating astern propulsion". 
 
When in sight of one another in a narrow channel or fairway: 
 
(i) a vessel intending to overtake another shall indicate her intention by the following signals 
on her whistle:

- two prolonged blasts followed by one short blast to mean "I intend to overtake you on your 
starboard side"; 
 
- two prolonged blasts followed by two short blasts to mean "I intend to overtake you on your 
port side". 
 
(ii) the vessel about to be overtaken shall indicate her agreement by the following signal on 
her whistle: 
 
- one prolonged, one short, one prolonged and one short blast, in that order. 
 
(d) When vessels in sight of one another are approaching each other and from any cause 
either vessel fails to understand the intentions or actions of the other, or is in doubt whether 
sufficient action is being taken by the other to avoid collision, the vessel in doubt shall 
immediately indicate such doubt by giving at least five short and rapid blasts on the whistle. 
Such signal may be supplemented by a light signal of at least five short and rapid flashes. 
 
(e) A vessel nearing a bend or an area of the channel of fairway where other vessels may 
be obscured by an intervening obstruction shall sound one prolonged blast. Such signal 
shall be answered with a prolonged blast by any approaching vessel that may be within 
hearing around the bend or behind the intervening obstruction. 
 
In or near an area of restricted visibility, whether by day or night, the signals prescribed in 
this Rule shall be used as follows: 
 
(a) A power-driven vessel making way through the water shall sound at intervals of not more 
than 2 minutes one prolonged blast. 
 
(b) A power-driven vessel underway but stopped and making no way through the water shall 
sound at intervals of not more than 2 minutes two prolonged blasts in succession with an 
interval of about 2 seconds between them. 
 
(c) A vessel not under command, a vessel restricted in her ability to manoeuvre, a vessel 
constrained by her draught, a sailing vessel, a vessel engaged in fishing and a vessel 
engaged in towing or pushing another vessel shall, instead of the signals prescribed in 
paragraphs (a) or (b) of this Rule, sound at intervals of not more than 2 minutes three blasts 
in succession, namely one prolonged followed by two short blasts. 
 
(d) A vessel engaged in fishing, when at anchor, and a vessel restricted in her ability to 
manoeuvre when carrying out her work at anchor, shall instead of the signals prescribed in 
paragraph (g) of this Rule sound the signal prescribed in paragraph (c) of this Rule. 
 
(e) A vessel towed or if more than one vessel is towed the last vessel of the tow, if manned, 
shall at intervals of not more than 2 minutes sound four blasts in succession, namely one 
prolonged followed by three short blasts. When practicable, this signal shall be made 
immediately after the signal made by the towing vessel. 
 
(f) When a pushing vessel and a vessel being pushed ahead are rigidly connected in a 
composite unit they shall be regarded as a power-driven vessel and shall give the signals 
prescribed in paragraphs (a) or (b) or this Rule.

(g) A vessel at anchor shall at intervals of not more than one minute ring the bell rapidly for 
about 5 seconds. In a vessel of 100 m of more in length the bell shall be sounded in the 
forepart of the vessel and immediately after the ringing of the bell the gong shall be sounded 
rapidly for about 5 seconds in the after part of the vessel. A vessel at anchor may in addition 
sound three short blasts in succession, namely one short, one prolonged and one short 
blast, to give warning of her position and of the possibility of collision to an approaching 
vessel. 
 
(h) A vessel aground shall give the bell signal and if required the gong signal prescribed in 
paragraph (g) of this Rule and shall, in addition, give three separate and distinct strokes on 
the bell immediately before and after the rapid ringing of the bell. A vessel aground may in 
addition sound an appropriate whistle signal. 
 
(i) A vessel of 12 m or more but less than 20 m in length shall not be obliged to give the bell 
signals prescribed in paragraphs (g) and (h) of this Rule. However, if she does not, she shall 
make some other efficient sound signal at intervals of not more than 2 minutes. 
 
(j) A vessel of less than 12 m in length shall not be obliged to give the above-mentioned 
signals but, if she does not, shall make some other efficient sound signal at intervals of not 
more than 2 minutes. 
 
(k) A pilot vessel when engaged on pilotage duty may in addition to the signals prescribed 
in paragraphs (a), (b) or (g) of this Rule sound an identity signal consisting of four short 
blasts. 
 
9.23. COLLISION AVOIDANCE IN CROATIAN PORTS AND MARITIME 
AREAS OF INTERNAL WATERS (COLREGs PART VI.) 
 
For the avoidance of collisions at sea in Croatian ports and areas of internal sea water in 
which the navigation is not very convenient, vessels are required to follow the COLREGs 
regulations in movement (arriving, leaving and passing) in the ports, during entering the 
ports in the semicircle of one nautical mile, measured from the entrance in ports on the rivers 
and their estuaries, and in narrow channels that are in the internal waters except for certain 
areas of marine water if it is not regulated by the special regulations. 
  
Small vessels, including boats, regardless of the type of propulsion give-way to larger 
vessels in ports and marinas as referred to in Article 41 of the Regulations. 
 
Ships entering a port, river or narrow channel must not interfere with the maneuvering of 
ships leaving the port, river or channel. 
 
If ships that have an advantage in maneuvering believe that other ships are not acting 
according to these rules, they must alert them with at least five short and rapid blasts. 
 
Fishing and trawler boats are not allowed in the ports to fish with light, and in other areas 
can use only shielded lights so as not to disturb a good and clear view of ships operating in 
these areas. If those ships or boats, its light interfere with visibility, are required at a given 
signal by the ship, from a long whistle sound, shut down or block their lights.

Ships and boats while sailing in internal waters and territorial sea of the Republic of Croatia 
may not approach the coast, as follows: 
 
1. ships that perform public transport, at a distance of less than 300 m;  
2. vessels for sport and recreation, at a distance of less than 200 m;  
3. motor boats and sailing boats at a distance less than 50 m. 
 
Rowing boats can sail at a distance less than 50m from the shore. 
 
In the water area next to beach the boat must sail at a distance greater than 50 m from the 
fences of the beaches, and 150 m from the natural beach. 
 
Powerboats must keep at a distance greater than 300 meters from the coast and in the area 
where it is not forbidden to them to navigate.

10.  SAFETY AT SEA 
 
10.1. FIRE 
 
Fire is one of the biggest dangers on board. The most common causes of fire and explosion 
on board are: 
Carelessness of the crew 
Electricity (faulty cables, plugs, overload) 
Flammable gases 
Self-igniting cargo or materials not properly secured or handled. 
 
Fire should be extinguish decisively and effectively at its initial stage. When the fire spread, 
it is far more difficult to extinguish it. Therefore, we should take the most effective measures 
using fire extinguishers, blankets, quilts, water (sea), without looking at the damage that will 
cause fire during firefighting (time is important). 
The development and spread of a fire on board depends on the airflow. Therefore, as soon 
as possible try to disable the air flow by closing windows and doors. 
The firefighting regularly depends on the location and type of burning material. The fire on 
the electrical system must not be extinguished with water, but with carbon dioxide or powder. 
Before working with an unknown fire extinguisher read simple guidelines. 
In the process of burning gases are formed that are deadly to humans in high concentrations. 
Try to keep low and avoid inhaling of toxic gases and smoke. 
 
10.2. FLOODING 
 
Flooding of the boat, if possible, should be stopped as soon as possible to avoid listing, 
overturning or sinking of the boat. 
Penetration of water, depending on the location and size, can be stopped or reduced with 
sealers or the stoppers from the inside. Manoeuvre boat as soon as possible to the mainland 
into the shallows so that the ship would not sink into the deep sea. Use all pumps and pump 
for discharge water from the ship must because additional mass of water into the boat 
creates a free surface that makes the boat unstable. 
Boat leader must regularly review the intake valves for engine cooling, cleaning toilets and 
other valves and connections that are installed on the underwater hull. 
 
10.3. MAN/PERSON OVERBOARD 
 
Immediately after spotting a person in the sea it is necessary to throw a life ring. In this way, 
it marks the place where it was MOB was noticed, and if the person manages to reach it it 
can keep him on surface. Maneuvers that boat can take are: 
- immediate turnaround (Anderson's turn) 
- Williams and turnaround 
- Y-backing. 
 
Anderson’s turn is performed when we notice the person falling overboard, by vessels with 
smaller turning circle and with shorter stopping distance (smaller vessels or those that are 
sailing with slower speed). This manoeuvre is initiated after the notification; the rudder is 
turned to the side on which we noticed the man overboard to make full circle around MOB.

After the ship has changed course to about 250° from original, the rudder is placed in the 
middle, and the engine put in reverse (astern) to stop the ship. 
 
Williams' turn is used when we do not know where the person was lost. This manoeuvre 
changes it course to 180° opposite from original course. Navigate slowly and cautiously, and 
maintain good watch. 
 
Y-backing is manoeuvre used by vessels with large turning circle and with good astern 
propulsion. We start this manoeuvre by shifting the vessel to one side and moving astern to 
the other side we rotate bow in new, opposite course from original (by 180°). In shape of 
letter Y.  
 
       
 
 
Picture – MOB manouvers 
 
10.4. ABANDONMENT 
 
Vessel should be abandoned only as last resort, when it is no longer safe for us to stay on 
the vessel. 
Not every damage to the ship is good enough reason to leave the ship. Even if vessel is 
aground or partially flooded it is still safer than being in life raft or in water with lifejackets at 
the open sea. 
If you have time, send a distress call to attract the attention of search and rescue team and 
ships in the vicinity in order for them to know the exact location of the ship in distress. Help 
the wounded, children and elderlies. Pick up anything that might help for survival such as 
drinkable water, portable marine radio, flares, rockets and smoke signals, blankets and 
thermal bags, food, first aid kit, chart of the area, marine and personal documents and more, 
if time permits. 
If conditions permit it is better to stay close to location of accident especially if the coastal 
station has received accident location. Preserve peace and stay calm.

10.5. LIFE - SAVING APPLIANCES 
 
Rafts can be rigid or inflatable. Life rafts differ in the number of people they can fit, at least 
6 up to 150 people. Rafts lighter than 185 kg are thrown into the sea by hand, heavier with 
special cranes. Depending on the area of navigation there are two variants of equipment, 
SOLAS pack A (with food and water) and pack B (without food and water). In the event that 
life raft is inflated upside down, one person should be sufficient to overturn life raft on the 
right side. Life raft on the boat or yacht are secured with hydrostatic hook that automatically 
releases life raft container if the vessel sinks. Rafts and hydrostatic hooks require regular 
maintenance. Life rafts are checked annually by the authorized service center, while the 
hydrostatic hook should be replaced every two years. 
 
 
Life rafts, especially pneumatic, are the 
most suitable for rescue in case of leaving 
the boat. When it is not activated, it takes up 
little space, and are relatively lightweight 
and easily descend into the sea. 
The raft has two circular tubes, each of 
which can independently keep a raft on the 
surface of water. Above the inner tubes we 
have canopy. Raft are inflated with gas from 
the cylinder at the bottom of the raft, and 
can be inflated by hand pump. Life raft has 
various equipment for survival. Activation of 
life raft is extremely simple - pulling the 
activation rope (painter line). Before you

10.6. LIFEBUOYS 
 
Lifebuoys are used for rescuing individuals, and are made of plastic or 
other material with good floating properties. On the buoy it is usually 
written the name of the ship and port of registry. Some models are 
equipped with smoke or light signal and rope with length 27,5m. 
Number of lifebuoys on board depends on the purpose and size of the 
boat and it is determined by the SOLAS Convention. 
 
 
 
10.7. LIFE JACKETS 
 
An indispensable piece of equipment of each boat is a life jacket which should be sufficient 
for all crew members and passengers. Be sure to have a number of children's vests. Allows 
a person in the sea to retain the correct position with the head and chest up, and turning 
mouth of a wearer above the water even if it is not conscious. The process of putting on 
must be practiced so that the user is able to put it quickly in case of need. The vest should 
be of very visible color, with reflective stripes, and must have a whistle and a flashing light. 
Preferably with belt for connecting under the crotch. 
 
Life jackets can have a rigid and self-inflated buoyancy. Self-inflatable comes with ampoules 
with CO2 gas which is activated by pulling the cord and the vest is inflated. We have two 
chamber, one main one reserve. The vest has inflation tube. 
 
 
 
10.8. TERMAL PROTECTIVE SUITS 
 
Thermal protective suits serve to prevent the loss of body heat when floating in cold water 
or room of the boat to survive in cold regions. 
 
Each suit must meet the following basic requirements: 
 
- 
shall indicate that the you wear it over warm clothing 
- 
Thermal protection quality must be such that the temperature of the body after one 
hour spent in a water at 5° C does not fall more than 2° C 
- 
if the material of suit is with their own insulation, body temperature must not fall more 
than 2° C after 6 hours in water at 0° C 
 
Some suits have integrated life-jackets, but if they do not, you need to wear a life jacket over 
suit.

10.9. EPIRB, SART and PLB 
 
EPIRB (Emergency Positioning Indicating Radio Beacon) is a radiotracer, radio buoy that 
manually or automatically initiates signalling of the place of distress. Based on this signal 
satellite system determines the position of the EPIRB and sends it to the SAR team. 
The device is activated in an emergency manually or automatically by a hydrostatic 
mechanism, which releases the device from the vessel if it is immersed in the water by few 
meters (in the event of sinking). The device transmits on 406.025 MHz type of emission 
G1B, together with their homing signal on the frequency 121.5 MHz. Capacity of the power 
source must be at least 48 hours, and on shell of the device should be printed instructions 
how to operate it, battery life and hydrostatic senses. The device must be placed so that is 
not moving and it cannot be activated in any conditions of ship (other than emergency) and 
so it can be free to rise to the surface in case of sinking of the vessel and in contact with 
water be activated. Each EPIRB has a built-in ID. 
 
There are three types of buoys: 
 
1. EPIRB VHF with a frequency 156.525 MHz (channel 70) 
2. INMARSAT EPIRB with frequency 1.6 GHz necessarily incorporating reception of the 
satellite navigation system GPS 
3. COSPAS / SARSAT EPIRB transmitting on frequency 406.025 distress signal and at 
the frequency 121.5 MHz for locating and guiding. 
 
In addition to these buoys specifically designed for marine industry there are two types of 
transmitters: 
 
1. 
ELT - Emergency Locator Transmitter intended for aviation and 
2. 
PLB - Personal Locator Beacon intended for land and individuals. 
 
SART (Search And Rescue Transponder) is a device that allows you to be located in an 
accident on the screens of radars with pointing direction on the screen with a series of 12 
points at an equal distance from one another. This device is mandatory piece of equipment 
within the GMDSS system. 
 
Radar transponder operates at a frequency of 9 GHz (9.2-9.5), and the X-radar area (3 cm). 
It has two modes: passive operation in a state of expectation of the radar pulse and power 
supply allows operation for a period of 96 hours and actively broadcast when it is initiated 
by radar and power supply allows operation for a period of 8 hours.

10.10. DISTRESS SIGNALS  
 
• The shot with firearm or other explosive signal, 
• Any continuous tone signaller in the mist, 
• Missiles or sprinklers that throw red stars fired one after another in short intervals, 
• Radio signal or any other signalling system comprising of a group . . . - - -. . .  SOS Morse 
signs, 
• Radio telephony signal that consists of words MAYDAY, 
• The danger signal NC (November Charlie), 
• Signal consisting of a square flag under which we have ball, 
• Open fires on board (e.g. urning barrels of oil, petroleum) 
• The rocket parachute flare or handflare that provides red light, 
• Smoke signal that emits smoke signal, 
• Waving outstretched and open arms up and down, 
• Radio-telegraph alert, 
• Radio phone alarm signal, 
• Orange colored piece of canvas, or a quadrilateral with a black circle or other appropriate 
symbol for identification from the air, 
• Dye markers for sea, 
• The signal for positioning emitted by emergency radio buoys – EPIRB 
• Approved signals emitted through radio communication system - GMDSS 
• Three or more small fires next to one another on land 
 
The use or displayed of any of the listed signals, except for the purpose of distress and the 
need for help, is prohibited.

11. MARITIME COMMUNICATIONS - RADIOTELEPHONY 
 
11.1. INTRODUCTION 
 
Maritime communications involve communications between ships, ships and coastal radio 
stations (CRS) as well as communication within and around the ship in case of mooring and 
unmooring, loading and unloading and similar.  
Ships and CRS can communicate by radio telephony, satellite, digital selective calling (DSC) 
and radio telex. 
Categories of messages that can be transmitted and received are called "priorities". The 
priorities indicate the importance of the message. 
1. DISTRESS ALERT - danger; indicates that there is a serious and direct threat to the 
ship, crew and passengers. Call usually begins with words "MAYDAY" 
2. URGENCY - EMERGENCY; indicates the existence of a serious danger to the ship, 
crew and passengers. It usually begins as a "PAN PAN" message. 
3. SAFETY - SAFETY; indicates the existence of imminent risk to navigation. Safety 
message usually begins with words "SECURITE". 
4. Routine - Regular; routine messages relating to communication within and outside 
the vessel, the exchange of data on maritime operations, communications ships and 
VTS (Master's, Pilot cells) and similar. 
 
The Global Maritime Distress and Safety - GMDSS came into force in 1999. as part of the 
International Convention SOLAS. The main goal of this system is to prevent accidents or 
mitigate their severe consequences, through Marine Safety Information - MSI, urgent 
informing and alerting the search and rescue (SAR), which coordinates aid and rescue. 
GMDSS system board enables communication with the coast station or other ships at any 
time and under any conditions. Communication by GMDSS includes: 
1. transmitting calls in case of distress to the coastal station, including the location of 
the vessel in distress; 
2. reception alert coast - ship; 
3. transmission and reception ship to ship; 
4. transmission and reception of messages relating to search and rescue operations 
(SAR) as well as On-Scene, direct communication at the scene during SAR 
operations; 
5. transmitting and receiving radio signals that indicate or determine the position of the 
ship; 
6. sending and receiving instant messages and security alerts (Maritime Safety 
Information Broadcast - MSI); 
7. internship communication, which means communication between ships. 
 
11.2. RADIO - TELEPHONY 
 
VHF (very high frequency) transceiver (transceiver = Transmitter + Receiver) is a device 
which emits and receives radio signals. VHF is used for radio communication over short 
distances, easy to use, and is used in navigation as in the territorial and internal waters and 
on the open seas. 
The receiver is used with a single button to talk (Push-to-talk button). If the installation is 
carried out as a "simplex" radio, speaking and listening cannot be performed simultaneously.

When we want to talk switch is pressed, and when we want to hear - let go of it. Before 
changing the voice to listen say the word "over" or "reception". 
If the installation is "duplex" radio, speaking and listening are possible simultaneously. Most 
coastal radio stations (ORP) are equipped with semi-duplex installation. 
VHF radio telephone has a maximum range of up to 40Nm depending on antenna height 
and power output. 
Reception of the radio signal does not always have the best quality and range of these 
devices will not always reach a maximum range. This means that the safety of the ship and 
crew could be compromised. These shortcomings in communication led to the introduction 
of DSC in maritime communications. 
 
Every ship and boat, which owns radio station has an identification mark. Call sign is a 
combination of letters and numbers. The first two characters indicate nationality (9A-
Croatia), while the other four indicate the ship in question because each ship has its own 
combination. 
Before transmitting messages or calls on channel 16 VHFa we need to wait for other users 
to finish. As soon as the channel is free we can make short and clear call for example: 
 
Office Šibenik, Office Šibenik, Office Šibenik 
THIS IS MOTOR BOAT "MARE", "MARE", "MARE" 
OVER 
 
After sending out invites wait one minute to answer: 
 
"MARE", "MARE", "MARE" 
HERE'S OFFICE ŠIBENIK (x3) 
GO TO CHANNEL 10 (transfer to channel 10) 
OVER  
 
This message must be confirmed, and then move on to the agreed working channel because 
channel 16 is reserved for emergency calls and contact.

VHF has two modes, High and Low. High is used in navigation outside the port and marina 
while Low is used for communication in ports because of our transmission may interfere with 
other devices around us. During port stay, VHF should be monitored while MF/HW should 
be switched off. 
 
11.3. VTS 
 
The system for monitoring and management of maritime transport (Vessel Traffic Monitoring 
and Information System) (hereinafter VTMIS System) is a complex technical and information 
system for monitoring, managing and organizing the entire maritime shipping in internal 
waters, territorial sea and protected ecological and fishing zone of the Croatia, which 
consists of marine coastal systems of automatic identification of ships, naval radar system 
(VTS Radar system), maritime radio-communication system, and other systems that provide 
insight into navigational circumstances at sea and creates interaction with the participants 
of maritime transport. 
The system of maritime transport (Vessel Traffic System - VTS) consists of a group of 
Croatian and international regulations governing the safe navigation of internal waters, 
territorial sea and protected ecological and fishing zone of the Croatia implemented by 
VTMIS. 
According to the Maritime Code: 
Supervision and management of maritime transport is carried out in order to improve 
maritime safety, efficiency of maritime transport and protection of the marine environment, 
and includes: 
1. collection of data on marine and maritime transport, 
2. providing data on maritime facilities, 
3. providing navigational advice and support to navigation of maritime facilities, 
4. organization of navigation and maritime traffic management. 
 
11.4. VHF DSC DEVICE 
 
VHF transceiver is designed for use in the maritime frequency band. Frequency range is 
from 156.025 to 163,275MHz which includes all international channels. The transmitter 
should have support for DSC calls in distress, calls to all ships, as well as individual and 
group calls. Some devices allow storage of incoming DCS calls and store their own DSC 
call directory. 
Other options may be scanning all channels, scanning priority channel, channel scan 
memory, select the channel 16 by a single action. Some models have alphanumeric keys 
on the microphone. 
VHF DSC device is used for calling (individual or group). Works on VHF channel 70. In 
recent times VHF DSC device comes already integrated. It is very useful in case of 
abandonment as pressing a single button sends distress to all ships in our VHF DSC range. 
Our DSC device will transmit a distress message until a ship does not confirm 
(Acknowledge) it, thus interruptes our call for help, but takes it upon himself to inform the 
rescue center. 
The station should have a MMSI so to be identified in the search and rescue. MMSI coastal 
station is different from the ship as it contains two zeros at the beginning. 
Before using the device it is required to obtain a license to use from the competent authority.
